records.cite: Katz et al. Dynamics of Adaptation During Three Years of Evolution Under Long-Term Stationary Phase. Mol Biol Evol (2021). DOI:10.1093/molbev/msab067.
records.snip: Five independent LTSP populations were established, each by inoculating ~5 x 10^6 E. coli K12 strain MG1655 cells/ml of Luria broth (LB)... the populations initially grew to ~10^10 cells/ml, during the first day of the experiment.
records.notes: Only paper of the four batch with an organism-level, computable growth. Explicit numbers: inoculum ~5e6 cells/ml; peak ~1e10 cells/ml reached 'during the first day' (~24 h). mu = ln(N/N0)/t = ln(1e10/5e6)/24 h = ln(2000)/24 = 7.601/24 = 0.317 /h. doubling_time = ln2/mu = 0.693/0.317 = 2.19 h. This is an approximate AVERAGE (lower-bound) rate because peak may have been reached in <24 h and includes lag; hence confidence low. 37C, 225 rpm, aerated flask, batch, aerobic all stated. LB composition not given in paper -> composition:[] , medium_composition_stated:false (not fabricated). No substrate uptake or product secretion fluxes reported.

records.strain: K-12 MG1655 pCS-λ (luciferase reporter plasmid, kanamycin selection)
records.cite: Held et al. Nutrient colimitation is a quantitative, dynamic property of microbial populations. Proc Natl Acad Sci U S A (2024). DOI:10.1073/pnas.2400
records.snip: The blue surface is a model fit to all replicate measurements (Poisson arrival time model with g_max ≈ 0.66 per hour, a_glu ≈ 23 per hour per mM glucose, a_amm ≈ 90 per hour per mM ammonium; R_glu,min ≈ 0.023 mM, R_amm,min ≈ 0.0039 mM).
records.notes: gmax = 0.66/h is the saturating (max) per-capita growth rate fitted from luminescence growth curves of E. coli K-12 MG1655 pCS-λ across glucose(0-2 mM) x NH4Cl(0-1 mM) gradients in modified M9 at 37°C (Poisson arrival-time model; Materials and Methods, 'Measurement of Growth Rates from Luminescence Growth Curves'). Growth quantified by linear regression of log-luminescence over the exponential interval, hence growth_method=calculated. doubling_time = ln2/gmax = 0.693/0.66 = 1.05 h (calculated). Other fitted Monod-type parameters: glucose affinity a_glu ≈ 23 /h/mM, ammonium affinity a_amm ≈ 90 /h/mM, minimum resource concentrations R_glu,min ≈ 0.023 mM, R_amm,min ≈ 0.0039 mM — these are affinities, not uptake fluxes, so uptake_rates left empty (no mmol/gDW/h derivable). No substrate uptake or product secretion fluxes are reported. A SEPARATE growth-YIELD experiment (same paper) used E. coli K-12 MG1655 (chromosomal GFP) in a slightly different modified M9 base [Na2HPO4 6 g/L, KH2PO4 3 g/L, NaCl 0.5 g/L, MgSO4 1 mM, CaCl2 0.1 mM] with glucose(0-40 mM) x NH4Cl(0-40 mM), 37°C, 500 RPM shaking, 24 h, OD600 in Biotek LogPhase 600; it reports biomass yields only in arbitrary OD units (no gDW-based Yxs and no growth rate), so it is not emitted as a flux/rate record. pH not stated (phosphate-buffered M9, ~7). Reference conditions cited in text (not this strain's own growth): typical M9 = 0.2% glucose ≈ 11.1 mM glucose + 18.7 mM ammonium.

records.med: Native desert biocrust soil hydrated with simulated rain (30% v/v 2H2O heavy water)
records.cite: Imminger et al. Survival and rapid resuscitation permit limited productivity in desert microbial communities. Nature Communications (2024). DOI:10.103

sec.method: reported; measured CO2 release from hydrated biocrust (whole-community, per g soil, not per gDW). Dry-state respiration 0.61 ng C/g/h

records.snip: calculating replication times for a heterotrophic metabolism yielded a median doubling time of 5.6 days, ranging from as short as 7 h to as long as 147 days ... High respiration rates after hydration were confirmed by measuring CO2 release
records.notes: Growth measured as single-cell 2H incorporation from 30% 2H2O over 24 h, converted to specific growth rate mu and cellular replication (doubling) time. Reported median doubling time under a heterotrophic-metabolism assumption = 5.6 days. Converted: td = 5.6 d x 24 = 134.4 h; mu = ln2/td = 0.693147/134.4 = 0.00516 /h. Reported range 7 h to 147 days (mu 0.0000196-0.099 /h). CO2 secretion is a whole-community rate per gram soil (3720 ng C/g/h hydrated vs 0.61 ng C/g/h dry); cannot be converted to mmol/gDW/h because biomass basis not given. No defined medium composition (native soil hydrated with heavy water). Not a single strain; mixed in situ community.

records.notes: Same physical condition as the heterotrophic record but replication times recomputed assuming chemoautotrophic (CO2-fixing) metabolism. Reported median doubling time = 18.7 days. Converted: td = 18.7 d x 24 = 448.8 h; mu = ln2/td = 0.693147/448.8 = 0.001545 /h. Reported range 2.1-471 days (mu 0.0000613-0.01375 /h). No defined medium; mixed in situ community; single-cell 2H2O/NanoSIMS.

records.med: CDM (chemically defined medium; CDM35/CDM47 series, amino-acid supplemented)
records.cite: Konstantinidis et al. Adaptive laboratory evolution of microbial co-cultures for improved metabolite secretion. Molecular Systems Biology (2021). DOI:

sec.method: author-estimated specific production rate from extracellular riboflavin titer after 72 h, assuming half the cell concentration derived from OD600=2 (Adler et al 2013 dependency)
LC-MS measured titer, parental monoculture in amino-acid-supplemented medium
UPLC measured titer, parental monoculture in amino-acid-supplemented medium

records.snip: For the parental strain, the lower bound of riboflavin production was set to the specific production rate of 6.13.10-6 mmol/(g CDW h) estimated from experimental data. The specific production rate was estimated from the extracellular ribofl
records.notes: Prokaryote = Lactobacillus plantarum (LAB); co-culture partner Saccharomyces cerevisiae is a yeast (eukaryote) and is NOT curated. Riboflavin specific secretion rate 6.13e-6 mmol/(g CDW h) is the authors' own estimate from the 72-h extracellular titer at OD600=2 (used as FBA lower bound). Measured titers also recorded: riboflavin 42 +/- 16.4 ng/mL, folate 48.3 +/- 10.5 ng/mL (parental). No absolute growth rate (h-1) reported in fulltext (growth rates shown only as figure scatter vs parental and used as FBA lower bounds); no glucose/substrate uptake rate reported. Medium = chemically defined CDM (CDM35-derived, e.g. CDM47 amino-acid supplemented); full composition only in Appendix Table, so composition[] and medium_composition_stated=false. Strain has no formal designation ('from our natural isolate collection'). Evolved isolates B4/E6 secreted more riboflavin (134, ~208 ng/mL) but their specific rates were left unconstrained/predicted by MARGE, so not curated as measured fluxes.

records.strain: lac reporter strain ("Loops" strain carrying operators O1/O2/O3 with VenusNB reporter)
records.cite: Chang et al. Robustness of DNA looping across multiple cell divisions in individual bacteria. Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences of the U
records.snip: Cells were cultured overnight in M9 medium with 0.4% glycerol as a carbon source ... The Loops strain ... exhibits significantly longer OFF intervals (OFF mean = 202 min, SE ± 10, or, on average, 2.8 cell cycles) ... >=40 h; 30 °C
records.notes: Single-cell E. coli lac-reporter strain in mother machine microfluidics, M9 + 0.4% glycerol, 30 °C. No growth rate stated directly; division (cell-cycle) time derived from OFF intervals reported in BOTH minutes and cell cycles: Loops strain 202 min = 2.8 cell cycles -> cell-cycle (doubling) time = 202/2.8 = 72.1 min = 1.20 h; mu = ln2/1.202 = 0.577 h-1. Consistency across other strains: No-loop 47 min/0.6 = 78.3 min; 1x repressor 178 min/2.6 = 68.5 min; 100x/Loops 292 min/4.5 = 64.9 min (division time ~65-78 min across strains). Medium only partially specified (0.4% glycerol carbon source; full M9 salt composition not given in main text -> medium_composition_stated=false). LOW confidence: doubling time is indirectly inferred from OFF-interval min:cell-cycle ratio, not a measured population growth rate; no flux data.

records.notes: Only quantitative growth datum in the paper: the doubling time of E. coli K-12 MG1655 in M9 minimal medium is stated as 'about' 60 min at 37°C (used to rationalize a 60-min phosphate-starvation incubation). doubling_time_h = 60 min / 60 = 1.0 h (reported, approximate). growth_rate_per_h mu = ln2/td = 0.6931/1.0 = 0.693 /h (calculated from the reported doubling time). records.snip: This system produced 1.38 g PHB with a PHB content of 83.0% of DCW at 120 h. The average PHB productivity during 120 h of operation was 11.5 mg h-1... The initial OD600 was 0.9, which corresponded to an initial DCW of 0.2 g DCW L-1... forma
records.notes: C. necator H16, formate as sole carbon source (from CO2 electrolysis), fed-batch 120 h, 30°C, air-purged (aerobic). GROWTH RATE: non-PHB biomass increased 0.40 g -> 0.56 g over first 24 h; mu = ln(0.56/0.40)/24 = ln(1.4)/24 = 0.3365/24 = 0.0140/h; td = ln2/0.0140 = 49.4 h (growth is formate-limited/near-stationary, PHB-accumulating). (OD-based apparent mu incl. PHB = ln(2.7/0.9)/48 = 0.023/h.) FORMATE UPTAKE: electrolyzer produces formate at 262 mg/h into 2 L; concentration held 0.26-1.0 g/L so consumption ~= supply = 262 mg/h volumetric. Total DCW at 120 h = 1.38 g PHB / 0.83 = 1.66 g in 2 L (0.83 g/L). Specific qFormate = 262 mg/h / 1.66 gDCW = 158 mg/gDCW/h; /46.03 g/mol = 3.42 mmol/gDCW/h. PHB: reported volumetric productivity 11.5 mg/h; specific = 11.5/1.66 = 6.9 mg/gDCW/h; /86.09 g/mol (C4H6O2 monomer) = 0.080 mmol/gDCW/h. YIELD: total formate supplied = 262 mg/h x 120 h = 31.44 g; Y(PHB/formate) = 1.38/31.44 = 0.044 g/g (approx; assumes near-complete formate consumption). Specific rates are approximate (biomass changes over run; used 120 h total DCW) -> confidence medium. pH not stated (catholyte 30 g/L KH2PO4 + 4 g/L K2HPO4 buffered). Seed culture (LB, 30°C, 250 rpm) reported without a growth rate, not tabulated.

records.snip: acidogenesis predominated in the presence of Fe(III) plaque, with butyrate accounting for 33.2% of carbon products compared to 20.8% in its absence. Acetate showed a similar increase (4.9% with Fe(III) plaque vs. 3.7% without) ... glucose o
records.notes: Iron-plaque (IP) condition. No growth rate/doubling time reported; strain grown to mid-log/steady state. Secretion values are CARBON FRACTIONS (% of product carbon relative to glucose consumed, per equation CCE=sum(C_metabolite x nC)/(C_glucose x 6)x100), not mmol/gDW/h. butyrate 33.2%, acetate 4.9%. Glucose 20 mM initial (batch). Fe(II) produced 2.11 +/- 0.06 to 2.24 +/- 0.17 mM by day 6 (ferrozine). glucose oxidation efficiency 22.2% (fraction of theoretical 24 mol e-/mol glucose); CCE ~94.3%. Nitrogenase activity 2006.19 +/- 64.89 nmol C2H4 mg protein-1 h-1 (acetylene reduction), and 1322.16 vs 1044.24 nmol in microcosm comparison. Full per-product mol/mol-glucose fluxes are in Supplementary Table S2, not present in XML. medium_composition_stated false because full Hoagland salt concentrations not given (only glucose 20 mM, pH 6.0).

records.snip: butyrate accounting for 33.2% of carbon products compared to 20.8% in its absence. Acetate showed a similar increase (4.9% with Fe(III) plaque vs. 3.7% without Fe(III) plaque).
records.notes: Plaque-free control (CK) condition. No growth rate/doubling time reported. Secretion values are CARBON FRACTIONS (% of product carbon), not mmol/gDW/h: butyrate 20.8%, acetate 3.7% (both lower than IP condition, consistent with more solventogenesis). Glucose 20 mM initial (batch). Under control, more solventogenic/unmetabolized products (hexose phosphate, pyruvate) accumulate; glucose oxidation efficiency and CCE lower than IP. Nitrogenase activity lower without plaque (e.g. 1695.49 +/- 108.12 nmol C2H4 mg protein-1 h-1; 1044.24 nmol in microcosm). Full per-product fluxes in Supplementary Table S2 (not in XML).

records.notes: Experimental monoculture growth rate mu=0.045 +/- 0.07 h-1 (reported; note the reported SD 0.07 exceeds the mean, likely a typo in source). doubling_time = ln2/mu = 0.693/0.045 = 15.4 h (calculated). Model iJB792 predicted mu=0.044 h-1 with the sucrose secretion constraint of 0.182 mmol/gDW/h, and mu=0.0539 h-1 unconstrained. Sucrose secretion 0.182 mmol/gDW/h is a literature-cited constraint (ref 33), not measured in this study. S. elongatus is a prokaryotic oxygenic cyanobacterium. 35 C and pH 8.9 taken from the stated BG-11 inoculum conditions (1 g/L HEPES pH 8.9, 100 mM NaCl, 35 C).

records.notes: Aggregate experimental growth-rate range across SPC prokaryotic members (phototroph + heterotroph): 0.04 +/- 0.01 to 0.06 +/- 0.015 h-1; per-organism values not individually tabulated in text (Fig 1b source data external). records.notes: Chemostat steady state: at steady state mu = D = 0.05 h^-1 (td = ln2/0.05 = 13.86 h, reported as ~14 h). Measured biomass yield on formate Yxs = 3.88 g CDW/mol formate (n=9 steady-state samples). Formate is sole carbon+energy source. Formate uptake qS derived: qS = mu/Yxs; convert Yxs to gCDW per mmol = 3.88/1000 = 0.00388 gCDW/mmol; qS = 0.05/0.00388 = 12.89 mmol formate/gCDW/h. No residual formate detected by HPLC (all consumed). No organic-acid secretion detected (HPLC screened lactate/acetate/propionate/isobutyrate/butyrate).

records.notes: Values explicit in Results (N. winogradskyi Nb-255 grown at 10 mM nitrite, pH 7.5). Reported: Vmax = 43.7 umol N/mg protein/h; Km(app) = 388.0 uM at test pH 7.5, and 19.2 uM at test pH 5.5 (same growth condition, different assay pH). Note umol/mg protein = mmol/g protein (identical numeric). O2 uptake DERIVED: from Eq.2 stoichiometry NO2- + 1/2 O2 -> NO3-, O2 = 0.5 x nitrite Vmax = 0.5 x 43.7 = 21.85 umol/mg protein/h. Nitrate secretion DERIVED: 1 nitrate per nitrite oxidized = 43.7 umol/mg protein/h. Could NOT convert to mmol/gDW/h: paper uses 5.7 g WET cell weight per g protein (not dry weight), so gDW conversion not derivable. No numeric growth rate (mu) or doubling time reported in main text (Fig 2 nitrite-oxidation activity in d-1 shown only graphically); growth stated qualitatively similar at 1 vs 10 mM at neutral pH. Temperature = optimal (Supplementary Table S1), not stated in main text -> null. Medium = DSMZ 756c, composition only in Supplementary -> composition empty, stated false. Vmax is maximum whole-cell nitrite-oxidation (catabolic uptake) activity, an appropriate substrate-uptake bound for GEM constraint.

Converted to per-hour: 0.104/24 = 0.004333/h. Doubling time reported 6.65 days = 6.65*24 = 159.6 h; check mu = ln2/td = 0.693/6.65 = 0.104/day, consistent. Substrate-specific uptake rates reported directly per mg protein per day (biomass-normalized): NO 20.93 +/- 5.1, formate 29.01 +/- 6.3 umol/mg protein/day; volumetric NO 1.69 +/- 0.1 and formate 2.38 +/- 0.2 mmol/day. Dedicated 10-day experiment (n=6): NO 1.57 +/- 0.02, formate 2.44 +/- 0.06 mmol/day; biomass 0.48 +/- 0.02 C-mmol/day; net yield Yxs = 0.48/2.44 = 0.197 ~ reported 0.20 +/- 0.01 C-mmol biomass per mmol formate. N2 secretion CALCULATED from stoichiometry 2 NO -> N2: 1.69/2 = 0.845 mmol N2/day (routine). CO2 secretion CALCULATED by carbon balance for 10-day run: formate C in (2.44) - biomass C (0.48) = 1.96 mmol C as CO2/day (paper states ~64% of formate linked to NO reduction/oxidation to CO2, ~20% assimilated to biomass, ~16% unaccounted). Conditions verbatim: 2.4 L working volume, 20 +/- 2 C, pH ~7.3 (1 M NaHCO3), stirred 500 rpm (two turbines), anoxic, NO sole e-acceptor. Single steady-state condition; routine-operation and 10-day-experiment rates emitted together as one record.

records.notes: Chemolithoautotrophic steady-state chemostat (membrane bioreactor). Experimental basis: dilution rate D = 0.006 h-1; at chemostat steady state mu = D = 0.006 h-1. Doubling time td = ln2/mu = 0.693/0.006 = 115.5 h (paper rounds to ~116 h). Nitrite uptake qS = 8.5 mmol NO2- gDW-1 h-1 measured during chemostat cultivation (this study); note Koch et al. reported ~8.7 for comparison. Nitrate secretion computed by stoichiometric mass balance from statement that nitrite is consumed stoichiometrically to nitrate => qNO3 ~ qNO2 = 8.5 mmol gDW-1 h-1. Cultivation: T=39 C, pH=7.7 (1 M KHCO3 buffer), stirred 150 rpm, DO ~30%, sparged Ar/CO2 (95:5) + air at 10 ml/min; CO2 sole carbon source (13C-bicarbonate tracer confirmed autotrophy). Medium = NOB mineral salts (Spieck & Lipski) with CaCO3 replaced by CaCl2.2H2O and trace-element mix listed above; base macro-salt (NaCl/MgSO4/KH2PO4 etc.) concentrations not numerically given in text (by reference). CO2 and O2 specific uptake and CO2-fixation fluxes appear only as model-predicted (FBA, Fig 3/Fig 4) values not stated numerically in text, so left null to avoid reporting model predictions as measured rates. Maintenance parameters: GAM 535 mmol ATP gDW-1, NGAM 0.90 mmol ATP gDW-1 h-1 (NGAM = FIG S1 y-intercept 0.90 mmol NO2- gDW-1 h-1 x model ATP yield 1 mmol ATP/mmol NO2-).

records.snip: mumaxO2 = 0.29 h-1 (s.d. = 0.006) ... VmaxO2 = 0.72 fmol O2 per cell per hour ... Vmax e-O2 = 2.9 fmol of electrons to O2 per cell per hour ... Km = 0.9 uM O2 (s.e. = 0.27)
records.notes: Aerobic batch growth of Cloacibacterium sp. CB-01 in GranuCult nutrient broth (meat peptone + meat extract), pH 7.3, 23 C. mumaxO2=0.29 h-1 estimated by nonlinear regression of measured aerobic respiration during unrestricted growth. Doubling time = ln2/mu = 0.6931/0.29 = 2.39 h (calculated). Reported O2 respiration Vmax=0.72 fmol O2 cell-1 h-1; converted to mass basis using measured CDW 108 fg cell-1: 0.72 fmol/108 fg = 6.67 mmol O2 gDW-1 h-1 (fmol/fg = mol/g). Electron flow to O2 = 2.9 fmol e- cell-1 h-1 (reported). Apparent half-saturation Km(O2)=0.9 uM. Growth yields (reported): Y_O2=4e14 cells mol-1 O2, Y_e-O2=1.0e14 cells mol-1 e- = 11+/-0.8 g CDW mol-1 e-. No secreted products quantified (respiratory CO2 not measured).

sec.method: calculated from stoichiometry N2O + 2e- + 2H+ -> N2 + H2O (1 N2 per N2O reduced): = qN2O = 0.66 fmol cell-1 h-1

yields.Y_e-N2O_gCDW_per_mol_e: 9.2 +/- 0.6 g CDW mol-1 e- to N2O (= 85% of Y_e-O2)

records.snip: mumaxN2O = 0.11 h-1 (s.d. = 0.001) ... VmaxN2O = 0.66 fmol N2O per cell per hour ... Vmax e-N2O = 1.3 fmol of electrons to N2O per cell per hour ... Km = 12.9 uM N2O (s.e. = 1.2)
records.notes: Anaerobic N2O-respiring batch growth of CB-01 (NNRB; reduces N2O to N2 via NosZII), same broth, pH 7.3, 23 C. mumaxN2O=0.11 h-1 by nonlinear regression. Doubling time = ln2/0.11 = 6.30 h (calculated). Vmax(N2O)=0.66 fmol N2O cell-1 h-1 (uptake of electron acceptor); mass basis = 0.66/108 fg = 6.11 mmol N2O gDW-1 h-1. Electron flow to N2O = 1.3 fmol e- cell-1 h-1. Secreted N2 computed from N2O+2e-+2H+->N2+H2O (1:1) => qN2=0.66 fmol cell-1 h-1 = 6.11 mmol gDW-1 h-1. Km(N2O)=12.9 uM. Yields (reported): Y_N2O=1.7e14 cells mol-1, Y_e-N2O=0.85e14 cells mol-1 e- = 9.2+/-0.6 g CDW mol-1 e-, which is 85% of aerobic Y_e-O2. Respiratory metabolism ~50% slower than aerobic.

records.snip: predicted a carbon consumption of 0.064 mol of inorganic carbon per 1 mol of ammonia ... 1.411 O2 + 0.064 CO2 + NH3 -> 0.953 H2O + 0.017 C3.85H6.69O1.78N + 0.983 NO2- + 0.983 H+
records.notes: Growth conditions: 42 C, pH ~7.2, closed-system batch, 2 mM ammonia, varying bicarbonate (standard A = 2 mM). Values here are MODELED stoichiometric coefficients from the thermodynamic growth equation (per 1 mol NH3), NOT measured specific fluxes and with no time base -> cannot convert to mmol/gDW/h. Model predicts 0.064 mol inorganic C / mol NH3 (=0.128 mol C / 2 mol NH3); experimental value used was 0.06 mol C/mol N. Biomass formula C3.85H6.69O1.78N at 0.017 mol/mol NH3. No measured growth rate or doubling time is reported in the extractable main text (growth followed via nitrite-production curves in Fig 1; full medium in Supplemental Methods, not in XML) -> growth_rate null, not fabricated. One representative record for standard condition A; conditions B-G differ only in carbon (0-0.1 mM) and ROS scavenger (catalase vs pyruvate).

records.notes: Continuous biofilm biotransformation. Two dilution phases: D=0.9 h^-1 for 3 h (wash out planktonic cells; OD600 fell from ~3 to ~0.3) then D=0.4 h^-1 for production (recorded). Reported volumetric productivity q_2-FMA = 83 +/- 2 umol/L/h (2-FMA secretion). Because product-forming biomass is a retained biofilm, D does NOT equal specific growth rate mu, so no mu recorded; biomass in gDW not reported so specific (mmol/gDW/h) rates not derivable. 3-FBz feed 10 mM accumulated to ~8 mM, 2-FMA peaked ~0.5 mM. Bioreactor temperature not restated in text (P. putida cultivated at 30 C elsewhere in the study); left null to avoid inventing. Batch-phase specific growth rates (mu) shown only graphically (Fig 3F) with no absolute numeric value in text (switch strain within 12% of control).

records.notes: CNB-2/DeltaLuxR is the faster-growing 'cheater' strain. Reported max per capita growth rate on 10 mM acetate MSM: beta_B = 0.75 h-1 (called 'experimentally determined growth rate of cheater', used in Fig 3B). doubling_time = ln2/0.75 = 0.92 h. CAVEAT/inconsistency: the Materials-and-Methods parameter block lists beta_max = 0.5 h-1 for the cheater, contradicting beta_B=0.75 in Fig 3B and the biology (cheater faster than cooperator); value taken as 0.75 h-1 per the explicitly 'experimentally determined' statement. These are Lotka-Volterra model per-capita rates derived from the paper's experimental system, not directly-tabulated batch mu, hence low confidence. Absolute effective growth rates vs inoculum density are only in Fig 1A/2A. No qS/qP reported.

records.notes: Catabolic-limitation series of E. coli NCM3722-derived titration strains. Medium = M9 minimal + 2 g/l glucose, 37C, shaking (aerobic), batch (deep-well plates). Discrete per-condition growth rates (mu), specific glucose uptake and acetate secretion rates were measured (uptake/secretion by linear regression of extracellular metabolite vs OD600-derived CDW using 1 OD600 = 0.413 g CDW/l, and growth rate) across a continuum of inducer levels, but the numeric values are presented only in Appendix figures / Dataset EV (images / external xls) and are NOT present in the article XML text, so no single mu/qS/qP number could be transcribed. Medium composition rows for phosphate/sulfate/ammonium are taken from the explicitly stated modified/15N-reference M9 (42.2 mM Na2HPO4, 22 mM KH2PO4, 8.56 mM Na2SO4, 11.3 mM NH4Cl); trace elements/Mg were by reference (Kochanowski et al.) and not enumerated. No arithmetic performed (no explicit input numbers for rates).

records.notes: Anabolic-limitation series (GOGAT titration) of E. coli NCM3722 derivatives; M9 + 2 g/l glucose, 37C, shaking (aerobic), batch. Explicit qualitative/relative numbers in text: anabolic limitation caused up to fivefold growth-rate reduction (no absolute mu given, so mu left null); up to ~50% of consumed glucose secreted as acetate (product yield Y_ace/glc up to ~0.5, mol/mol on consumed glucose per the wording); besides acetate and 2-oxoglutarate, all other quantified metabolites were secreted at <70 umol/gCDW/h. Discrete per-level mu, qGlucose, qAcetate, qOxoglutarate values exist but only in Appendix figures/Dataset EV (images/xls) outside the XML text, so no absolute rates transcribed. No arithmetic performed (no explicit paired numerator/denominator numbers available in text).

records.snip: For experiments under oxidative stress, a continuous flow of 440 ul/h of 3% hydrogen peroxide (Sigma H1009) was added to obtain a constant concentration of 500 uM in the fermenter medium.
records.notes: Same chemostat as control: 200 ml working volume, 70 ml/h LB flow; added H2O2 flow 440 ul/h is negligible relative to 70 ml/h, so D ~= 70.44/200 = 0.352 h-1 (reported as 0.35). At steady state mu = D = 0.35 h-1 (calculated); td = ln2/0.35 = 1.98 h. H2O2 held at 500 uM. LB component concentrations not stated -> medium_composition_stated=false. No uptake/secretion fluxes reported.

records.strain: MG1655 (fluorescent-labeled derivatives strains 1-4; ancestors 0Y attTN7::pRNA1_mVenus and 0R attTN7::pRpsL_mKate2Hyb, carrying ampicillin-resistance plasmids)
records.cite: Pacciani-Mori et al. Constrained proteome allocation affects coexistence in models of competitive microbial communities. The ISME Journal (2021). DOI:
records.snip: the typical growth rate of the strains, estimated from growth curves measured in the same experimental conditions used for the competition assays, is 0.3 1/h. The competition assays started from a cellular density of ~8 · 10^6 cells/mL ...
records.notes: Growth rate 0.3 1/h reported directly ('typical growth rate of the strains ... is 0.3 1/h'), measured from growth curves in the same M63 + 1% w/v glucose + ampicillin conditions, 30 C, 1050 rpm, aerobic (porous rayon film). doubling_time = ln2/mu = 0.6931/0.3 = 2.31 h (calculated). Medium named M63 minimal medium; only glucose (1% w/v) and ampicillin (concentration not stated) explicitly given; base M63 salt composition not enumerated in the paper. Same 0.3 1/h applies to all four fluorescent strains (identical MG1655 genetic background). No qS derivable: glucose is sole carbon source but no biomass yield or per-cell glucose consumption reported (density given only as cells/mL: start ~8e6, saturation ~1e9). No secretion rates or yields reported. NOTE: a maximum growth rate g_max ~1.2 1/h for E. coli MG1655 at 30 C in rich medium with glucose is cited from Van Derlinden and Van Impe (ref 50), not measured in this paper, so not recorded.

records.notes: mu=0.59±0.01 h-1 reported. Doubling time computed td=ln2/mu=0.6931/0.59=1.175 h. records.snip: the microcosms operated independently in chemostat mode with a defined mineral salt medium (70 uM K2HPO4, 700 uM KNO3, 100 uM MgSO4, 100 uM CaCl2, 100 uM NaCl) plus trace elements ... at dilution rate of 0.1 day-1 (1.25 ml.min-1).
records.notes: Chemostat steady-state growth rate derived as mu = D. Stated D = 0.1 day-1 = 0.1/24 h = 0.004167 h-1. Doubling time td = ln2/mu = 0.693/0.004167 = 166.4 h (=6.93 days). D confirmed by flow/volume: 1.25 ml/min x 1440 min/day = 1800 ml/day = 1.8 L/day; 1.8 L / 18 L = 0.1 day-1. Carbon/energy source = methane (4.9% in air); N source = nitrate (N-limited). Gas consumption/production rates (CH4, O2 uptake; CO2 output) are computed by the authors from input-output gas concentrations x flow rate but appear only graphically in Fig 2 with no numeric values in the main text and are not normalized to biomass (gDW), so specific fluxes (mmol/gDW/h) cannot be computed. Organism is a mixed methanotrophic enrichment community, not a single strain; value flagged low confidence. Temperature 20C stated. pH variable across phases; 6.5 buffer imposed in phase IV (control continuous-methane condition reported here). This mu=D applies to steady-state (control/continuous) operation; cycled-treatment chemostats had the same D but periodic 20-day CH4 on/off forcing (not strict steady state).

records.snip: When grown in monoculture, B. thetaiotaomicron produced succinate, lactate, formate, and acetate during fermentation, which decreased the pH from 6.7 to 5.0. At 20 h, B. thetaiotaomicron reached its maximal density of 1.2 x 10^6 viable cell
records.notes: Batch anaerobic monoculture in WC (no mucin). Growth reported as viable cell counts by flow cytometry (live/dead SYBR Green/PI), NOT OD or gDW; max 1.2e6 viable cells/ul at 20 h; density then declined after glucose/pyruvate depletion due to pH drop 6.7->5.0. No specific growth rate (mu) or doubling time reported; cannot be computed because initial viable cell density (cells/ul) at t=0 is not stated (inoculum only given as 1 ml of OD600 0.1 preculture into 60 ml). qS/qP not computable: substrate and product concentrations are shown only in Fig. 2a with no tabulated numeric values, and biomass is in cells/ul not gDW. Products succinate/lactate/formate/acetate are HPLC-identified; propionate not detected (medium lacks vitamin B12). WC (Oxoid) full composition not stated in paper; only glucose and pyruvate named as main carbon sources. temperature 37 C, 170 rpm stirring, initial pH 6.7.

records.notes: Batch anaerobic monoculture in WC (no mucin). Growth = viable cells/ul by flow cytometry. Max 7.8e5 viable cells/ul at 24 h; pH 6.7->5.8 at 24 h. After glucose/pyruvate depletion at 24 h, enters slow-growth (diauxic) mode consuming acetate+lactate to make butyrate, pH rising 5.7->6.0; viable cells 1.8-3.8e5 from 40-120 h (two experiments). No mu or doubling time reported; not computable (no t=0 viable-cell density; substrate/product values only in figures; biomass in cells/ul not gDW). Diauxic shift corroborated by RNA-seq up-regulation of lactate dehydrogenase (RIL182_00335, 01719) and butyryl-CoA:acetate CoA-transferase (RIL182_00413) between 12 and 48 h. WC full composition not stated; glucose+pyruvate named as carbon sources. 37 C, 170 rpm, initial pH 6.7.

records.cite: Windels et al. Antibiotic dose and nutrient availability differentially drive the evolution of antibiotic resistance and persistence. The ISME Journal
records.snip: We set psi_max=0.6 h-1, which corresponds to an average of one division per cell per 30 min (at AB=0 ug/ml and n=1) for the ancestor ... in accordance with our experimentally measured killing rate ... psi_min=-10 h-1 ... at AB=400 ug/ml.
records.notes: E. coli SX43 ancestor maximum growth. Paper states psi_max = 0.6 h-1, where psi is defined as change in log10(cell count) per hour, and 'corresponds to an average of one division per cell per 30 min' at zero antibiotic and full nutrient (n=1). Doubling time td = 30 min = 0.5 h (reported). mu (natural-log basis) = ln2/td = 0.6931/0.5 = 1.386 h-1 (calculated). Cross-check: mu = psi_max * ln(10) = 0.6 * 2.3026 = 1.382 h-1 (consistent). Killing rate psi_min = -10 h-1 (log10 basis) at 400 ug/ml amikacin corresponds to a drop of ~ -6 log10 units/h (biphasic first-phase kill of non-persisters); this is a death rate, not a growth/substrate flux, so not emitted as an uptake/secretion record. Value set as a model parameter but explicitly grounded in the authors' experimental time-kill/growth data. MHB is a complex undefined broth; composition not stated in paper (medium_composition_stated=false). No substrate uptake or product secretion fluxes reported.

records.cite: Basan et al. Overflow metabolism in E. coli results from efficient proteome allocation. Nature (2015). DOI:10.1038/nature15765. [PMC4843128]

sec.method: reported (slope of acetate vs OD600 x growth rate; exponential fit for OD600<0.3, td=18 min)
reported (alternative exponential fit for OD600 0.3-0.5, td=28 min)

records.snip: Assuming exponential growth for OD600 data below 0.3 and alternatively from 0.3 for 0.5 gave doubling time of 18 min and 28 min, respectively. The corresponding acetate excretion rates were 14.3 and 3.6 mM/OD600/hr. These data should be reg
records.notes: E. coli K-12 (NCM3722 parent strain, wild-type) grown on LB in well-shaken aerobic batch culture (Extended Data Fig. 1 note). Two exponential-fit estimates for the same LB culture: (1) OD600<0.3: doubling time td=18 min=0.30 h -> mu=ln2/td=0.6931/0.30=2.31 h-1 (calculated), acetate excretion 14.3 mM/OD600/h (reported); (2) OD600 0.3-0.5: td=28 min=0.4667 h -> mu=0.6931/0.4667=1.485 h-1 (calculated), acetate 3.6 mM/OD600/h (reported). growth_rate_per_h field carries the early-phase value (2.31 h-1); the late-phase alternative (1.485 h-1) is documented here. Both acetate rates emitted as separate secretion entries. Authors explicitly label these semi-quantitative because LB batch growth is non-steady. Temperature 37 C per the study's bioreactor/standard E. coli conditions (batch temperature not restated in text). LB (Miller) composition not stated in this paper (medium_composition_stated=false). Note: the paper's main quantitative dataset (Fig.1 acetate excretion vs growth rate across glycolytic carbon sources in MOPS minimal medium; bioreactor glucose uptake, O2, CO2 and acetate in Extended Data Fig.3a) resides in figure source-data/images and is not numerically present in the article text, so those per-condition uptake/secretion fluxes could not be transcribed without fabrication. Text-stated derived quantities not tied to a single culture (not emitted as records): acetate-line threshold growth rate lambda_ac ~= 0.76 h-1 (55 min/doubling), below which acetate excretion vanishes; proteome efficiencies epsilon_f ~= 750 and epsilon_r ~= 390 mM ATP/OD600/h. Minimal-medium base (MOPS-buffered, Cayley et al.): 40 mM MOPS, 4 mM tricine (pH 7.4 KOH), 0.1 M NaCl, 10 mM NH4Cl, 1.32 mM KH2PO4, 0.523 mM MgCl2, 0.276 mM Na2SO4, 0.1 mM FeSO4 + Neidhardt trace micronutrients; carbon-source concentrations were given only in figure tables (images), so no minimal-medium growth-rate record could be built.

records.snip: ...0.94 doublings h-1 in glucose...to rescale the time axis to units of generations
records.notes: Paper reports the mixed (wildtype-like) E. coli MG1655 population growth in the turbidostat as 0.94 doublings h-1 in glucose. Converted to specific growth rate mu = doublings/h * ln2 = 0.94 * 0.6931 = 0.6515 h-1. Doubling time = 1/(doublings per h) = 1/0.94 = 1.064 h. Value is for the pooled CRISPRi library reference population (negC/wildtype-like); no substrate uptake or product secretion fluxes reported. Full M9 salt composition not given in text; only carbon source (0.4% glucose), kanamycin (35 ug/mL) and pH 6.5 stated. Population is aneuploid CRISPRi strain but WT-like at reference guide.

records.notes: Chemostat steady-state community growth rate = imposed dilution rate D = 0.103 h-1, so mu = 0.103 h-1 (mu = D at steady state); Td = 6.7 h (ln2/0.103 = 6.73 h, consistent). Flow F = 51.56 mL/h (F/D = 500 mL implied working volume; assembly stated as 400 mL). Temperature 37 C and pH 7.15 +/- 0.15 automatically controlled; microaerophilic 2% O2. Serum varied 0/5/50% v/v (medium variant, does not change D). Community-level rate; per-species specific rates not reported. Acid consumption and metabolomics reported graphically only (no numeric flux).

records.notes: Kinetic/stoichiometric characterization of an Accumulibacter Clade IIA enrichment in an SBR under cyclic anaerobic-feast/aerobic-famine EBPR operation. This record = ANAEROBIC FEAST phase. Rates are verbatim reported values, calculated by the authors as linear rates of change of each analyte normalized to VSS (volatile suspended solids, biomass proxy) and Clade IIA relative abundance. No specific growth rate (mu) reported. Medium not given in-paper (cites Garcia Martin et al. 2006), so composition:[] and stated:false. VSS used instead of gDW. PHB synthesis (7.0) is intracellular storage, included as a product flux; PHB degradation belongs to aerobic phase (separate record). Ca uptake/release and PHV negligible, not reported. Units retained verbatim as C-/P-/Mg-/K-mmol per gVSS per h.

records.cite: Chiarelli et al. Single-Inclusion Kinetics of Chlamydia trachomatis Development. mSystems (2020). DOI:10.1128/mSystems.00689-20. [PMC7567582]
records.snip: Simulations of the two models ... using a multiplicity of infection (MOI) of 1 and an RB generation time of 2.27 h produced results that mimic the general kinetics of the chlamydial cycle ... 1.13 h (half the measured RB doubling time) ...
records.notes: Measured RB (reticulate body) doubling/generation time = 2.27 h at 37°C (the paper labels 1.13 h and 4.54 h as half and 2x the 'measured RB doubling time', confirming 2.27 h is the measured value). Growth rate calculated: μ=ln2/td=0.693/2.27=0.305 h−1. C. trachomatis is an obligate intracellular pathogen grown in Cos-7 host cells, so there is no defined bacterial medium and no measured substrate uptake/secretion fluxes. Slower/faster replication observed at 35°C/40°C respectively but no numeric doubling times reported for those temperatures.

records.cite: Balagaddé et al. A synthetic Escherichia coli predator-prey ecosystem. Molecular Systems Biology (2008). DOI:10.1038/msb.2008.24. [PMC2387235]
records.snip: Conversely, the prey grew to a high density (∼35 000 cells per reactor) in ∼10 h ... precultures were then used to seed microchemostat reactors with ∼20 cells nl−1 ... dilution rate (D)=0.1125 h−1
records.notes: Reactor volume ~9 nL; seeded at ~20 cells/nL => ~180 cells initial. Prey reached ~35,000 cells/reactor in ~10 h during initial growth phase. Apparent NET specific growth rate mu_net = ln(35000/180)/10 = ln(194.4)/10 = 5.270/10 = 0.527 h^-1. Because continuous dilution (D=0.1125 h^-1) removes cells during this phase, gross mu = mu_net + D = 0.527 + 0.1125 = 0.640 h^-1; doubling time = ln2/0.640 = 1.08 h. Estimate confounded by dilution and predation onset; approximate cell counts and time. No substrate uptake or product secretion fluxes reported (LBK is a rich, undefined-carbon medium).

records.cite: Balagaddé et al. A synthetic Escherichia coli predator-prey ecosystem. Molecular Systems Biology (2008). DOI:10.1038/msb.2008.24. [PMC2387235]
records.snip: The high prey density led to rescue of the predator population, which recovered from ∼5000 cells per reactor to >20 000 cells per reactor in ∼10 h ... dilution rate (D)=0.1125 h−1
records.notes: Predator recovered from ~5,000 to >20,000 cells/reactor in ~10 h (rescue phase). Apparent NET mu = ln(20000/5000)/10 = ln(4)/10 = 1.386/10 = 0.1386 h^-1. Correcting for dilution: gross mu = 0.1386 + D(0.1125) = 0.251 h^-1; doubling time = ln2/0.251 = 2.76 h. Heavily confounded: this is a CcdA-antidote rescue recovery, not steady exponential growth; counts/time approximate. No metabolic flux data reported.

records.cite: Balagaddé et al. A synthetic Escherichia coli predator-prey ecosystem. Molecular Systems Biology (2008). DOI:10.1038/msb.2008.24. [PMC2387235]
records.snip: Figure 3A illustrates coexistence dynamics at D=0.11 h−1 ... an increase in D (to 0.2 h−1) led to a phase of damped oscillatory dynamics with much shorter periods (∼50 h).
records.notes: Continuous co-culture operated at dilution rates D=0.11 and 0.2 h^-1. Populations are oscillatory (not at simple single-species steady state), so mu is NOT equal to D and no specific growth rate is reported; dilution_rate captured as an operational condition. IPTG varied 0-50 µM across runs (oscillation elicited at IPTG >=5 µM). No substrate uptake or product secretion fluxes reported.

records.cite: Balagaddé et al. A synthetic Escherichia coli predator-prey ecosystem. Molecular Systems Biology (2008). DOI:10.1038/msb.2008.24. [PMC2387235]
records.snip: These populations initially coexist at a low dilution rate (D=0.15 h−1). They switched to damped oscillations with short periods (∼30 h) upon an increase in D (to 0.23 h−1). Further increase in D (to 0.31 h−1) at ∼250 h led to predator wash
records.notes: Continuous co-culture at dilution rates D=0.15, 0.23, 0.31 h^-1. Predator washed out at D=0.31 h^-1 (implies max predator specific growth rate < 0.31 h^-1 under these conditions, an upper bound only). Oscillatory dynamics => mu != D; no measured specific growth rate. No metabolic flux data reported.

records.cite: Schreck et al. Impact of crowding on the diversity of expanding populations. Proc Natl Acad Sci U S A (2023). DOI:10.1073/pnas.2208361120. [PMC1008916
records.snip: Colonies starting from single cells were grown on plates with LB and 2% agar at 37 °C for 30 h up to a population size between 10^8 and 5·10^8 cells.
records.notes: Paper reports NO explicit growth rate value for E. coli (only 'consistent with previously reported values'). Population-average specific growth rate derived from the two explicit endpoints: start N0=1 cell, final N=10^8 to 5x10^8 cells, t=30 h. mu=ln(N/N0)/t. Lower bound: ln(1e8)/30 = 18.421/30 = 0.614 /h; upper bound: ln(5e8)/30 = 20.030/30 = 0.668 /h; midpoint reported = 0.64 /h. doubling_time = ln2/mu = 0.693/0.64 = 1.08 h. CAVEAT: this is a colony-expansion population average on solid agar, NOT an exponential-phase mu (colony growth is growth-layer-limited and non-exponential), and includes lag; treat as a rough order-of-magnitude average only, hence low confidence. No substrate uptake or product secretion fluxes are reported (physics/evolution study of clone-size distributions). LB composition not itemized in paper. A well-mixed control (~50 cells in 200 uL LB, incubated overnight to 10^8-10^9 cells) is reported but 'overnight' duration is unspecified so no rate is derivable. The color-switching budding yeast (S. cerevisiae yJK10, YPD, 30 C microfluidic) is a eukaryote and excluded.

Text explicitly reports direction: lactate consumed, acetate produced, glucose NOT consumed (glucose uptake set to 0). No growth rate/doubling time reported (growth not shown numerically for D. piger). Metabolite magnitudes are in Fig. 1f only, not in text -> qS/qP not computable without fabrication; left null. Sulfate reduction and H2S production are implied by organism physiology and medium sulfate content but were not quantified here (faecal H2S measured in vivo elsewhere, not a rate).

records.snip: co-cultures of F. prausnitzii with D. piger promoted fermentation of glucose and production of lactate and butyrate (Fig. 1f). F. prausnitzii produces butyrate through the butyryl-CoA:acetate CoA-transferase pathway and accordingly, acetate
records.notes: Batch anaerobic CO-CULTURE in defined mPGM, 24 h, 37C, pH 7.2. Growth of F. prausnitzii significantly increased in co-culture vs monoculture (CFU, Fig. 1e, P=0.0003) but no mu/doubling time given. Directional flux is stated in text: glucose consumed (fermented), lactate + butyrate produced, acetate net-unchanged (produced by D. piger from lactate then consumed by F. prausnitzii for butyrate synthesis). Cross-feeding model (Fig. 1g): D. piger consumes lactate (electron sink) -> acetate -> F. prausnitzii uses acetate + glucose -> butyrate. All metabolite magnitudes are Fig. 1f only and absent from text; qS/qP not computable without fabricating numbers, so rates left null except acetate net accumulation ~0 and glucose-uptake=0 cases that are stated in prose. Only P-values are given in text.

records.notes: CSTR steady state; dilution rate D = Fin/V = 40 ml/h / 200 ml = 0.2 h-1 (residence time 5 h). At chemostat steady state mu = D = 0.2 h-1 for the persisting species. Kp persists at steady state in all 8 glucose conditions (0.25-5 mM), so mu_Kp = 0.2 h-1. Doubling time td = ln2/mu = 0.6931/0.2 = 3.47 h. Medium salt-mix components (NaCl 17.6, CaCl2 0.4, MgCl2.6H2O 0.4, MnCl2.4H2O 0.2, CoCl2.6H2O 0.2 g/L) given as 20x stock used at 1x, so final = stock/20 (e.g. NaCl 17.6/20=0.88 g/L). Strain MGH-78578 is the genome-scale model strain (iYL1228); experimental K. pneumoniae strain not separately named. No experimental qS/qP tabulated in text (uptake/secretion are FBA-derived intracellular fluxes; model vmax=10 mmol/gCDW/h, Km=0.01 mM are E. coli literature parameters, not measured).

records.notes: Batch SIP microcosm, mixed 3-member consortium. Acetate 50 mM = 3000 mg/L (implies acetate MW=60). Consumed = 3000-280 = 2720 mg/L over 408 h = 2720/60 = 45.33 mM; volumetric qS = 45.33/408 = 0.111 mmol/L/h. CH4 = 17.5 mL/microcosm STP over 408 h; liquid volume ~20 mL; 17.5 mL/22400 mL/mol = 0.7813 mmol per microcosm; /0.020 L = 39.06 mmol/L; /408 h = 0.0957 mmol/L/h. Reported community biomass yield Yxs = 0.01 g-VSS/g-acetate (=0.015 g-COD/g-COD, assuming protein/biomass 0.5 and VSS/COD 1.42). Theoretical acetate->methane conversion 88%. Community pFBA model gives 0.706 mol ATP/mol acetate and predicts DTU068_1 consumes 1 mol acetate -> 1 mol formate intracellularly (0.824 mol oxidized via FdhA-NuoEF, 0.045 mol formate excreted via FdhA-FdxH-CytB) plus H2 + CO2; these are model predictions, not measured fluxes. Per-organism specific rates (mmol/gDW/h) not measured. Growth rate not directly measured; doubling time not derivable from batch data reported.

records.notes: Growth rate measured by single-cell microscopy (exponential fit to cell length over time), hence growth_method=calculated. Reported single-cell growth-rate range = 0.010-0.017 min-1. Converted to per-hour: 0.010 min-1 x 60 = 0.60 h-1; 0.017 min-1 x 60 = 1.02 h-1 -> mu = 0.60-1.02 h-1. Doubling time td = ln2/mu: at 0.010 min-1, td = 0.6931/0.010 = 69.3 min = 1.155 h; at 0.017 min-1, td = 0.6931/0.017 = 40.8 min = 0.68 h -> td = 0.68-1.16 h. (Model illustration used non-measured example rates 0.005 and 0.02 min-1 for switching-probability calculation; not used here as they are model inputs, not measured growth.) Note: cost of full lac operon expression lowered growth rate ~6% (uninduced vs fully induced), not treated as a separate condition. No substrate uptake, product secretion, or yield values are reported in this paper (it measures fluorescence/switching, not metabolite fluxes). Carbon source = succinate 0.4% (w/v). Thiamine 30 mM and uracil 10 mM reported verbatim from Methods (Bacterial strains and culture conditions).

records.notes: GEM/dynamic-modeling paper with real triplicate anaerobic batch cultures of F. succinogenes S85 on 3 g/L glucose, 39C, 100% CO2 (Hungate). Reduced core model reported as 72% higher (0.137*1.72=0.236/h). Product/biomass yields are model EFM yield boundaries from Table 2 (mmol product per mmol glucose; biomass in g/mmol). All uptake fluxes normalized so glucose=1. records.snip: L.B. EX_O2 (mmol/gDW/h) −11.5; U.B. EX_Ac (mmol/gDW/h) 3; δ (h–1) 0.03; V M Glc (mmol/gDW/h) 10; K M Glc (mM) 0.01; V M Ac (mmol/gDW/h) 10... generation time of 20 min
records.notes: MODELING PAPER — values are dynamic-FBA model constraints, NOT measurements made in this study. Growth rate from assumed generation time 20 min: mu = ln2/(20/60 h) = 2.079 h-1; doubling = 0.333 h. O2 uptake (-11.5) and acetate secretion max (3 mmol/gDW/h) were calibrated to Varma & Palsson 1994 (ref 18) E. coli aerobic glucose batch data. Glucose/acetate Vmax=10 mmol/gDW/h with KM=0.01 mM are model MM parameters (from literature). Temperature/pH not stated. medium_composition_stated=false (M9 base not itemized). Flagged low confidence because provenance is secondary/model bounds.

up.method: FBA/model-derived; glucose uptake (EX_cpd00027_e0) fixed at -0.0447 mmol/gDW/h to represent minimal (non-growth) glucose consumption for maintenance under a zero-growth simulation, NOT the uptake at the 0.0231 h-1 growth condition

records.snip: biomass production flux (vbiomass) was fixed at 0.0231 h-1 ... ec-iTP251 accurately predicted experimentally observed growth rates of T. pallidum for both glucose and pyruvate uptakes ... The glucose uptake rate (EX_cpd00027_e0) was fixed a
records.notes: Growth rate: paper states experimentally observed growth rate of T. pallidum was captured by ec-iTP251 for glucose and pyruvate; the biomass flux (vbiomass) was fixed at 0.0231 h-1, i.e. the experimental specific growth rate mu=0.0231 h-1 (reported). Doubling time CALCULATED: td=ln2/mu=0.693/0.0231=30.0 h. Carbon sources evaluated as sole carbon source in silico: glucose, pyruvate, mannose (mannose growth-rate prediction described as realistic vs glucose). Medium: experimental growth derives from the Edmondson et al. 2018 coculture (modified CMRL 1066 + 20% FBS with Sf1Ep rabbit epithelial cells); full component composition not enumerated in this paper, so medium_composition_stated=false. Glucose uptake -0.0447 mmol/gDW/h is explicitly the minimal NON-GROWTH maintenance consumption (zero-growth simulation to derive NGAM), not the qS at mu=0.0231 h-1; recorded with that caveat. Energy parameters (context, not yields): GAM=48.69 mmol/gCDW/h and NGAM=1.50 mmol ATP/gDW/h via Pirt equation; biomass molecular weight standardized to 1 g/mmol. Temperature, pH and O2 tension of the experimental culture are not numerically stated in this paper (oxygen inferred microaerophilic from the organism's biology). Highest proteome allocation glucose 29.6% (not a flux). Substrate-specific growth-rate bars (Fig 4D) are model predictions, not independent experimental measurements, so a single experimental growth-rate record is emitted.

No numeric maximum specific growth rate, doubling time, qS, or qP is given in the main-text XML: growth curves, substrate consumption and VFA (C2-C6,C8) production are shown only graphically in Fig. 4/Fig. 6 and max specific growth rate calculation is in Supplementary Materials & Methods / Supplementary Fig. S3B (not in this file). records.notes: Methanotrophic (reverse-methanogenesis) growth. mu = ln2/td; reported td = 29 d = 29*24 = 696 h; mu = 0.6931/696 = 9.96e-4 /h. Doubling_time_h = 696. records.snip: Biomass in the planktonic phase increased at a steady rate with a maximum growth rate of 0.23 d-1 and an average doubling time of ~3 days (Fig. 1B).
records.notes: Growth rate reported by authors via R 'growthrates' logistic model. Reported mu = 0.23 d-1; convert to per-hour: 0.23/24 = 0.00958 h-1 ~= 0.0096 h-1. Cross-check with reported doubling time ~3 d = 72 h: mu = ln2/72 h = 0.693/72 = 0.00963 h-1 (consistent). Dilution rate 0.022 h-1 stated in Table 1 (7.8 mL/h feed / ~350 mL = 0.0223 h-1). Note: planktonic doubling (~3 d) exceeds reactor dilution (~3.7 d) so this ecotype is retained. Temperature ~30 C (temperature-controlled room); pH ~7.2. Medium = 'lactate coculture medium (Supplementary Data 1)' sulfate-free; full composition only in supplement, not main text, so composition:[] and stated:false (pre-culture/reference cocultures used CCMA medium + 30 mM sodium DL-lactate; monocultures 5 mL CCMA + 10 mM Na-acetate + 30 psi H2). Yields (H2/lactate 1.8, CH4/lactate 0.4 mol/mol) are FBA model predictions, flagged as such. No absolute qS/qP in mmol/gDW/h given in text (only relative fluxes mMol/gDCW/h in Fig 4).

records.notes: Attached-phase biomass accumulation rate reported = 0.087 d-1 (via total sediment protein as biomass proxy, R 'growthrates' logistic model). Convert to per-hour: 0.087/24 = 0.003625 h-1 ~= 0.0036 h-1. Cross-check reported doubling ~8 d = 192 h: mu = ln2/192 = 0.693/192 = 0.00361 h-1 (consistent). This growth rate (~8 d doubling) is far below the reactor dilution rate 0.022 h-1 (~1.3 d), consistent with a sessile biofilm retained by attachment rather than washout. Same medium/temperature/pH as planktonic record. No absolute qS/qP reported; only relative fluxes (mMol/gDCW/h) from FBA model iSI1283.

records.snip: Trajectories of cell sizes from 265 independent E. coli experiments are shown in light gray lines and a typical trajectory is highlighted. The doubling time is approximately 20 min.
records.notes: This is a computational SDE-inference paper re-analyzing published mother-machine cell-size time series for E. coli, B. subtilis, D. discoideum, and S. pombe (data shared by Suckjoon Jun, Frank Bruggeman, Shigehiko Yumura, Yuichi Wakamoto). Only the E. coli generation/doubling time is stated quantitatively: 'The doubling time is approximately 20 min.' mu computed from doubling time: mu = ln2/td = 0.6931/(20/60 h) = 0.6931/0.3333 h = 2.08 /h. doubling_time_h = 20 min = 0.333 h. No medium composition, temperature, pH, or metabolic flux (uptake/secretion) values are reported in this paper (they reside in the original cited datasets). B. subtilis is also a prokaryote in this study but NO numeric doubling time or growth rate is given for it in the text (only qualitative 'sizer-adder' classification), so no B. subtilis record is emitted. S. pombe (yeast, td approx 2 h) and D. discoideum (amoeba) are eukaryotes and excluded. Confidence low: rate is approximate ('approximately 20 min') and no cultivation/medium metadata in-paper.

records.notes: Chemostat consortium (not a pure culture). At steady state, retained community grows at mu = dilution rate D = 0.026 h^-1 (paper: cells growing slower than D=0.026 h^-1 were washed out). Doubling time td = ln2/0.026 = 0.6931/0.026 = 26.66 h (calculated). Rates are VOLUMETRIC (µmol/L/h), NOT biomass-specific, because biomass (gDW) was not quantified — cannot convert to mmol/gDW/h. N2O reduction 0.13 µmol/L/h reported. Acetate uptake computed as D×Δacetate = 0.026×(5.0-4.15 mM)=22.1 µmol/L/h. N2 secretion inferred 1:1 from N2O reduction. Temperature 25 °C is inferred from the 25 °C used for all aqueous/gas Henry's-law conversions ('<49 nM in the aqueous phase at 25 °C'); not explicitly stated as incubation temperature. pH not stated (phosphate-buffered). Trace-metal (Table S1) and vitamin (Table S2) compositions are in supplementary files not available here.

records.notes: Growth rate reported directly as 0.04 +/- 0.01 h-1 (Fig S1) and doubling time 18 +/- 4 h (Fig 2C); consistent: mu = ln2/td = 0.693/18 = 0.0385 h-1. Formate uptake 19 +/- 2 mmol/gCDW/h measured directly. Biomass-formate yield Yxs = 2.8 +/- 0.8 gCDW/mol formate reported (Eq 1). Gross CO2 secretion derived: FDH oxidizes formate 1:1 to CO2, so gross CO2 = formate uptake = 19 mmol/gCDW/h; net is smaller due to Rubisco fixation (not given as single number). OD-to-biomass conversion used 0.4 +/- 0.1 gCDW/L per OD600. Base M9 salts (Na2HPO4/KH2PO4/NaCl/NH4Cl/MgSO4) not explicitly listed in paper; only trace elements + formate given.

records.notes: Chemostat: at steady state mu = D. This record = slowest end of the studied continuum, D=0.001 h-1 (stated minimum). Reported doubling time 30 d = 720 h (check: ln2/0.001 = 693 h = 28.9 d, consistent). Temp 65 C (all T. kivui cultures cultivated at 65 C). Medium adjusted to pH 7.0 with NaOH but reactor pH monitored stable at 6.0 during steady state -> pH field = 6.0 (reactor). Stirred 300 rpm. Cultures grown >=4 retention times before sampling. Catabolism: glucose -> 2 acetate + 2 CO2 via EMP+PFOR, plus WLP homoacetogenesis (CO2 -> acetate). Specific glucose-uptake and acetate-production rates are growth-rate-dependent and reported graphically (Fig. 5a,b) / Source Data, not as text numbers, so qS/qP left null.

records.snip: the growth kinetics of the cultures, assuming oxygen was the only limiting substrate, were determined by fitting a Monod kinetic model, resulting in a maximum growth rate (umax) of 0.11 h-1 (95% CI = 0.09 to 0.12) and a half-saturation cons
records.notes: Batch culture, 30C, 200 rpm, NMS medium + 5 uM CuCl2; 20% CH4 / 20% O2 headspace (N2 balance). Reported max growth rate umax = 0.11 h-1 (Monod fit, O2 as sole limiting substrate; 95% CI 0.09-0.12), Km(O2) = 1.69 mM. Doubling time = ln2/umax = 0.6931/0.11 = 6.30 h (calculated). Growth rate per condition computed by authors as log-linear regression of biomass vs time over exponential phase (~16-40 h). Uptake/excretion fluxes computed by authors (mmol/gDCW/h) via regression of metabolite vs biomass divided by growth rate; per-condition numeric values appear only in figures (Fig 2E, 5D) and are not in text. Uptake rates listed here are the FBA reference constraints stated in text: methane fixed at 10 mmol/gDCW/h in all analyses; example O2 flux 15 mmol/gDCW/h at optimal 1.5:1 O2:CH4 ratio; O2 exchange lower limit -10 mmol/gDCW/h. O2:CH4 specific-uptake ratio ~1.5:1 optimal; 1.2-1.4:1 at 10-25% initial O2; ~0.5:1 at 5% O2. GAM 59.8 mmol ATP/gDCW, NGAM 3.5 mmol ATP/gDCW/h. Full NMS recipe cited (ref 72), not stated, so medium_composition_stated=false; pH not stated.

records.snip: All serial transfer experiments were carried out in 10-mL cultures grown at 37 °C with vigorous shaking. The cultures were initiated by 1:100 dilution ... in maltose (500 μg/mL)-limited M9 medium (M9M). ... If we assume a generation time of
records.notes: Growth rate DERIVED from an ASSUMPTION, not a direct measurement: text states 'If we assume a generation time of 30 minutes' (i.e. doubling_time=0.5 h). mu=ln2/td=0.6931/0.5=1.386 h^-1. This 30-min generation time is a generic E. coli assumption used for a reversion-rate calculation, not a medium-specific measured value; maltose-limited growth is likely slower, hence low confidence. Maximum growth rates were actually measured by Bioscreen (Methods) but the numeric values appear only in supplementary figures (S5/S6) and S1 Data, not in the article text. Medium: Methods give base recipe '0.4% maltose, 1 mM MgSO4, 0.1 mM CaCl, 0.2% Thiamine' in M9 salts; serial-transfer experiments used maltose limited to 500 µg/mL. No qS or qP reported.

records.snip: The uptake rate (i.e., lower and upper bound) of CO2 and H2 were fixed at a 1:4 ratio, specifically at 1.20 and 5.11 mmol/gDW/h, while the minimum export rate of CH4 was fixed at 1.15 mmol/gDW/h.
records.notes: Community-level (not single-species) flux constraints for a thermophilic (55C) anaerobic H2/CO2-methanating biofilm community. H2 uptake 5.11 and CO2 uptake 1.20 mmol/gDW/h and CH4 export >=1.15 mmol/gDW/h were derived by the authors from the MEASURED input gas flow rate QIN [L/LR.d] converted to mmol/gDW/h using ideal-gas molar volume 24.46 L/mol (298 K) and an estimated biofilm dry mass per liter; CO2:H2 constrained to 1:4 stoichiometry. Input gas H2:CH4:CO2 = 62:23:15. No measured microbial growth rate is reported (member growth rates are FBA-simulated at 0.5x max community growth via cooperative tradeoff), so growth_rate left null. Medium = BA basal anaerobic (composition only in referenced protocol, not numeric in main text) -> medium_composition_stated false. Values are load-bearing GEM boundary conditions and are experimentally grounded via gas-flow measurement.

records.snip: 15N-FSRA identified 14 amino acids (minimum ratio > 0) that are available and taken up by intracellular Mtb from the macrophage ... Gln and Val are both used as nitrogen donors ... (Table 1 Flux Spectral Ranges)
records.notes: Rates are FSRA flux spectral ranges = amino-acid nitrogen uptake flux expressed RELATIVE to that amino acid's nitrogen content in biomass (dimensionless), NOT absolute mmol/gDW/h. Values transcribed verbatim from Table 1 (min, max, median). FSR>1 (Gln median 2.47, Val median 2.88) = uptake exceeds biomass need => net nitrogen donor; FSR~1 (Ala) = taken up and incorporated 1:1; FSR<1 (e.g. Asn 0.13) = partly de novo synthesized. Gln identified as predominant N donor. No absolute uptake rate, mu, or doubling time is reported (48 h isotopic steady-state labeling). Serine not taken up (min FSR 0) and serC (phosphoserine transaminase) auxotroph is attenuated in macrophages. In-vitro control: Mtb grown in 7H9 broth + 5% OADC + 0.5% glycerol, and Roisin's/Rosin's minimal medium with glycerol (C) + 10 mM NH4Cl (N) + 0.1% tyloxapol, 37°C, 150 rpm, aerobic - but no numeric mu/flux reported for that condition so no separate record made.

records.notes: E. coli S1030 host cells maintained in a chemostat for PACE. Dilution rate D = flow/volume = 400 mL/h / 250 mL = 1.6 /h. At chemostat steady state mu = D = 1.6 /h. Doubling time td = ln(2)/mu = 0.693/1.6 = 0.433 h (~26 min); feasible in this glucose+casamino-acids rich Davis medium at 37 C (below E. coli mu_max ~2 /h, no washout). Temperature 37 C taken from associated infection/culture steps. pH not stated for chemostat (theophylline-vehicle pH used only for lagoon equilibration in one experiment). Downstream lagoons operate at D=100/40=2.5 /h but host cells transit without net growth there (phage replicate), so not recorded as a bacterial growth condition. No substrate uptake or product secretion rates measured.

records.notes: E. coli MG1655 antibiotic-resistance experimental-evolution study, not a physiology/flux study. Medium supplements explicitly stated (casaminoacids 1 g/L, glucose 2 g/L, glycerol 5% v/v); the M9 base salt composition (Na2HPO4/KH2PO4/NaCl/NH4Cl/MgSO4/CaCl2) is NOT enumerated in the text. Growth conditions explicit: 30 C, 150 rpm orbital shaking, 96-well plates, aerobic, batch (1:5 or 1:50 serial dilution). Growth rates of drug-free evolved clones were reported (fitderiv/Swain 2016; mild-selection clones mean=10.59, s.e.=0.332, n=29; strong-selection clones mean=9.69, s.e.=0.199, n=89) but NO units are given in the article and the magnitude (~10) is not interpretable as a specific growth rate in h^-1 (E. coli mu on M9+glucose is ~0.6-1.0 /h); therefore growth_rate_per_h left null rather than fabricate a unit. No doubling time, no qS, no secretion/product fluxes, and no yields are reported. Also note separate handling steps at 30 C with 230 rpm and 14 h LB Broth-Lennox (Condalab) cultures for competition assays, but these carry no rate data either.

records.notes: Intrinsic (drug-free) per-capita growth rate of the sensitive ancestor REL607 reported in Table 1 as r_S = 0.0169 min^-1. Converted to per h: 0.0169 * 60 = 1.014 h^-1. Doubling time td = ln2/mu = 0.6931/1.014 = 0.684 h. Growth rate measured in bioreactor as media influx rate needed to hold constant density; time-averaged between 200-300 min post-drug at steady state (here drug=0). Medium = Davis Minimal Media (Sigma), full salt composition not enumerated in paper (commercial product) so medium_composition_stated=false; liquid overnight/working medium stated as Davis Minimal Media with 1,000 ug/mL glucose (paper prints '1,000 g/ml', a units typo for ug/mL; agar plates used 2,000 ug/mL). Bioreactor experiments run in a temperature-controlled warm room at 30 C. No substrate uptake or product secretion fluxes reported.

records.cite: Hansen et al. Antibiotics can be used to contain drug-resistant bacteria by maintaining sufficiently large sensitive populations. PLoS Biology (2020).
records.snip: r_R | Intrinsic per capita growth rate of drug-resistant strain | 0.0152 min^-1 ... The resistant isolate exhibits both increased resistance to doxycycline ... as well as decreased growth rate (small fitness cost).
records.notes: Intrinsic (drug-free) per-capita growth rate of the doxycycline-resistant REL606-derived mutant reported in Table 1 as r_R = 0.0152 min^-1. Converted to per h: 0.0152 * 60 = 0.912 h^-1. Doubling time td = ln2/mu = 0.6931/0.912 = 0.760 h. Value is ~10% below the sensitive strain (r_S=0.0169 min^-1), the reported small fitness cost. Same bioreactor/medium/temperature (Davis Minimal Media + ~1000 ug/mL glucose, 30 C) as the sensitive record; full Davis salt composition not enumerated so medium_composition_stated=false. No uptake/secretion fluxes reported.

records.cite: van der Meij et al. Streptomyces venezuelae uses secreted chitinases and a designated ABC transporter to support the competitive saprophytic catabolis

up.method: sole carbon and nitrogen source; consumption not quantified as a flux

sec.method: detected accumulating in spent supernatant of dasBC importer mutant by LC-MS; not quantified as a rate

records.snip: The minimal chitin medium contained, per liter: 5 g of chitin flakes derived from shrimp, 0.6 g of MgSO4.7H2O, 2 g of (NH4)2SO4 (when specified), 150 mL of NaH2PO4/K2HPO4 buffer (0.1 M, pH 6.8), 1 mL of trace elements solution, and 850 mL o
records.notes: Fully defined minimal chitin medium captured from Methods. Growth was assessed only by endpoint CFU counts (wild type ~10^8 CFU/mL vs ~10^6 CFU/mL for chitinase mutants after 6 days) and relative metabolic activity/sporulation - these are order-of-magnitude sporulation endpoints, not time-resolved exponential biomass, so no reliable specific growth rate (mu) or doubling time is derivable and none is reported; growth_rate left null rather than computed to avoid an unreliable value from spore-count endpoints. Substrate uptake (chitin) and product (chitobiose, LC-MS) are described qualitatively, not as fluxes.

up.method: sole carbon source; consumption not quantified as a flux

records.snip: Glucose medium consisted of 5 g of d-glucose and always included 2 g of (NH4)2SO4 as a nitrogen source, replacing the chitin in the minimal chitin medium. Cultures were generally grown in 5 mL volumes within 25 mL glass tubes and incubated
records.notes: Defined minimal glucose medium (5 g/L D-glucose + 2 g/L (NH4)2SO4, otherwise identical base to the minimal chitin medium: 0.6 g/L MgSO4.7H2O, 150 mL/L 0.1 M phosphate buffer pH 6.8, 1 mL/L trace elements, 850 mL/L tap water). Reported as a reference/comparison condition (S. venezuelae grew but was outperformed by chitin in metabolic activity and sporulation). No quantitative growth rate, doubling time, or uptake/secretion flux is reported or derivable (only endpoint biomass/CFU comparisons), so rate fields left null.

records.cite: Schmitz et al. The thermoacidophilic methanotroph Methylacidiphilum fumariolicum SolV oxidizes subatmospheric H2 with a high-affinity, membrane-associ

up.method: calculated from whole-cell Vmax(app)=340 nmol H2 min-1 mg DW-1 measured by MIMS (Km(app)=195±10 nM); 340 nmol/min/mgDW ×60 = 20400 nmol/h/mgDW = 20.4 mmol/gDW/h

records.snip: The maximum H2 oxidation rate (Vmax(app)) was measured to be 340 nmol H2 min−1 mg DW−1 and the cells have an apparent high affinity (Km(app)) for H2 of 195 ± 10 nM ... the cells used were cultivated in a chemostat under such conditions that
records.notes: Whole-cell maximum specific H2 oxidation (uptake) rate. Unit conversion: qH2 = 340 nmol H2 min-1 mgDW-1 × 60 min/h = 20400 nmol h-1 mgDW-1 = 20.4 µmol h-1 mgDW-1 = 20.4 mmol gDW-1 h-1. Km(app) for H2 = 195±10 nM (whole cells; text also cites 190±10 nM). Cells were H2-limited chemostat biomass (nitrate as N-source medium). Whole-cell kinetics measured at 50 °C (MIMS); growth temperature 55 °C. pH not restated for the 0.5 L reactor (main reactor pH 3.0). No CO2-fixation biomass yield or mu reported. This is a max oxidation capacity (Vmax), representing the H2 specific uptake ceiling, not necessarily the steady-state chemostat qH2.

records.cite: Okano et al. Regulation underlying hierarchical and simultaneous utilization of carbon substrates by flux sensors in Escherichia coli. Nat Microbiol (

up.method: calculated from reported total carbon-uptake flux on glycerol alone = 23 ℂ (ℂ=1 mM carbon-atoms per OD600 per hour) and DW conversion 1 OD600·mL = 0.44 mg DW; see notes
calculated: 23 mM C-atoms/(OD600·h) ÷ 0.44 gDW/(L·OD600) = 52.3 mmol C/gDW/h

yields.Yxs_note: derived: mu/qglycerol = 0.7 h-1 / 17.4 mmol gDW-1 h-1 = 0.040 gDW per mmol glycerol (0.0134 gDW per mmol C)

records.snip: the growth rate on glycerol only (≈ 0.7/h) ... the total carbon-uptake flux ... remained approximately constant ... at a level corresponding to that of growth on glycerol alone, (23 ± 1)ℂ (error represents 95% CI, n = 11) ... where ℂ =1 mM
records.notes: E. coli NCM3722 growth on glycerol as sole carbon source. mu ≈ 0.7 h-1 (reported). Doubling time td = ln2/mu = 0.693/0.7 = 0.99 h (calculated). Uptake: paper's flux unit ℂ = 1 mM carbon-atoms per OD600 per hour; total C-uptake on glycerol alone = 23±1 ℂ. Biomass conversion (Methods): 1 OD600·mL = 0.44 mg DW → OD600 of 1 = 0.44 gDW/L. Specific C-uptake = 23 mmol C L-1 h-1 (per OD600) ÷ 0.44 gDW L-1 = 52.3 mmol C/gDW/h. Glycerol has 3 C → glycerol uptake = 52.3/3 = 17.4 mmol glycerol/gDW/h. Threshold flux jth ≈ 25 ℂ for glucose (strain NQ1243) and lactose (strain NQ917) marks the crossover between hierarchical and simultaneous utilization; glucose-only and lactose-only mu not numerically stated (only 'never below 0.7/h' and crossover growth rate 0.7-0.8/h), so not emitted as separate records to avoid fabrication. Carbon substrates supplied at 120 mM C-atoms for growth-rate measurements; N-C- base salt composition given by reference (Ref 37), not enumerated in text. pH not stated.

records.cite: Mellbye et al. Genome-Scale, Constraint-Based Modeling of Nitrogen Oxide Fluxes during Coculture of Nitrosomonas europaea and Nitrobacter winogradskyi

up.method: initial 5 mM NH4+ (from 2.5 mM (NH4)2SO4) fully oxidized over the 10 h experiment; time course in Fig 1A, no numeric specific rate stated

sec.method: measured; 83.5 +/- 3.1 uM after 1 h then consumed by 4 h (concentration, not specific rate)
measured headspace peak 30.2 +/- 5.7 ppm during active NH3 oxidation
model simulation peak ~1 umol h-1 per 5 ml culture = 1 umol/h / 0.005 L = 0.2 mmol/L/h (dFBA-predicted maximum, not measured net rate)

records.snip: Experimental batch mineral salts medium for N. europaea contained 2.5 mM (NH4)2SO4, 0.75 mM MgSO4, 0.1 mM CaCl2, 12.5 mM KH2PO4, 1.25 mM NaH2PO4.H2O, 2.3 mM Na2CO3, and trace elements... pH... adjusted to 7.8.
records.notes: Batch medium composition fully stated (verbatim, above). Conditions: 30C, pH 7.8, 200 rpm, closed batch 10 h, OD600 0.2 inoculum, aerobic (sufficient O2 in headspace). No specific growth rate reported for the batch (OD600 measured but no slope/mu given) -> growth_rate null. Uptake substrate NH4+: initial 5 mM NH4+ (2.5 mM (NH4)2SO4 x2), consumed within experiment; numeric specific qNH4 not stated (time course only in Fig 1A). Secretion values are measured concentrations/peaks (NH2OH 83.5 uM at 1 h; NOx peak 30.2 ppm; N2O peak 42.1 ppm). NO 0.2 mmol/L/h derived from model-predicted max 1 umol/h per 5 ml (=0.005 L): 1e-3 mmol / 0.005 L / h = 0.2 mmol/L/h; this is a dFBA-simulated maximum, not a measured net flux. No gDW conversion available (OD only) so specific per-gDW rates not derivable.

records.snip: The medium for N. winogradskyi was the same formulation as the N. europaea medium except that it contained 5 mM NaNO2 instead of (NH4)2SO4. The pH... adjusted to 7.8. ...N. winogradskyi would need to produce NO at a maximum rate of approxim
records.notes: Batch medium identical to N. europaea recipe but with 5 mM NaNO2 replacing (NH4)2SO4 (verbatim). Conditions 30C, pH 7.8, 200 rpm, closed batch 10 h, OD600 0.05 inoculum, aerobic. No batch mu reported -> null. Substrate NO2- initial 5 mM, ~80% consumed by 4 h (Fig 2A). NO 0.026 mmol/L/h = 130 nmol/h / 5 ml (0.005 L): 0.13e-3 mmol /0.005 L/h = 0.026 mmol/L/h, model-inferred maximum not a measured net flux. NOx peak 1.5 ppm and N2O 0.08 ppm are measured headspace concentrations. No gDW conversion available.

records.notes: Coculture batch on N. europaea ammonium medium (verbatim recipe). Combined inoculum OD600 0.25, 30C, pH 7.8, 200 rpm, closed batch 10 h, aerobic (transient microaerobic from O2 competition). No mu reported for batch -> null. Substrate NH4+ 5 mM initial (2.5 mM (NH4)2SO4), NO2- an intermediate. Secretion peaks measured: NOx 53.7 ppm, N2O 47.8 ppm, NH2OH 54.4 uM at 1 h. All are measured concentrations/peaks, not specific per-gDW rates; no gDW conversion available. culture_mode = co-culture (two organisms in one batch vessel).

records.notes: Chemostat dilution rate D = feed/volume = 6 ml/h / 12 ml = 0.5 /h. At chemostat steady state mu = D = 0.5 /h. Doubling time = ln2/mu = 0.6931/0.5 = 1.386 h. Consistency check with reported generations: gen/h = D/ln2 = 0.5/0.6931 = 0.721; over 28 d = 672 h -> 0.721*672 = 485 generations, matching the reported 485 generations. Base lactate mineral medium composition is given only by reference (Paulick et al. 2009), hence medium_composition_stated=false; explicitly stated additions are 0.015 M sodium fumarate and 10 mg/l gentamicin. pH not stated. Oxygen classed microaerophilic because paper states oxygen can be limiting and fumarate is supplied as alternative electron acceptor.

records.med: modified AMP-A (autoclaved artificial seawater, N-replete), with 5 mM TAPs
records.cite: Szul et al. Carbon Fate and Flux in Prochlorococcus under Nitrogen Limitation. mSystems (2019). DOI:10.1128/mSystems.00254-18. [PMC6392094]
records.snip: 0.2 liters culture was transferred ... to four identical bottles containing 2.0 liters medium (2.2 liters final volume) and incubated for 4 days to reach log phase growth (mu ~ 0.44); the experiment then began on day 0 ...
records.notes: Log-phase specific growth rate reported as mu ~ 0.44 (units day^-1, inferred from paper's consistent use of day^-1: chemostat 0.09 day^-1 and 'one cell doubling per day' = 0.69 day^-1). Converted to per-hour: mu = 0.44/24 = 0.01833 h^-1. Doubling time = ln2/mu = 0.6931/0.44 = 1.575 day * 24 = 37.8 h (equivalently 0.6931/0.01833 = 37.8 h). Medium: AMP-A is autoclaved artificial seawater derived from AMP1 (ref 51) with (NH4)2SO4 400 uM replaced by NH4Cl 800 uM; experiment used modified AMP-A with 5 mM Tris-acetate-phosphates; inorganic carbon (bicarbonate) standard AMP starts at 0.6 mM. Full base-salt recipe not itemized in text (given by reference to AMP1/Turks Island Salt Mix), so composition list is partial. uptake_rates/secretion_rates left empty: total organic carbon (TOC) and particulate organic carbon (POC) 14C-photosynthesis (inorganic carbon fixation) rates and DOC/exudate release were measured but reported ONLY as bar graphs in Fig 1 relative comparisons (paper has 0 data tables); no absolute mmol/gDW/h or per-cell numeric rate is given in text, so no qS(HCO3-) or qP could be computed without inventing numbers. Qualitatively: N-replete noon carbon fixation was the higher (reference) condition, dropping >50% by 16:00.

records.med: modified N-limited AMP-A (artificial seawater, 5 mM TAPs, 16 uM NH4Cl); N-free basal = Turks Island Salt Mix
records.cite: Szul et al. Carbon Fate and Flux in Prochlorococcus under Nitrogen Limitation. mSystems (2019). DOI:10.1128/mSystems.00254-18. [PMC6392094]
records.snip: the dilution rate of the chemostat (which in steady state is the growth rate of the population) was well below the rate for one cell doubling per day (0.09 versus 0.69 day-1) ... Cultures ... destructively sampled 6 days after chemostat dil
records.notes: Chemostat dilution rate D ~ 0.09 day^-1 explicitly stated (replacing ~9% culture volume daily; also Fig S3: growth balanced to ~0.09 day^-1). At steady state mu = D, so mu = 0.09 day^-1 = 0.09/24 = 0.00375 h^-1. Doubling time = ln2/mu = 0.6931/0.09 = 7.70 day * 24 = 184.9 h (equivalently 0.6931/0.00375 = 184.8 h). Paper explicitly contrasts this with 'one cell doubling per day' = 0.69 day^-1 (ln2 per day), confirming day^-1 units. Limiting N: 16 uM NH4Cl (vs 800 uM in replete). uptake_rates/secretion_rates empty: N-limited total carbon fixation reported as <60% of replete at noon and total photosynthesis <half of replete, plus larger fraction of fixed carbon released as DOC/exudate - but all reported ONLY as relative Fig 1 bar graphs (0 data tables); no absolute mmol/gDW/h or per-cell numeric flux stated, so qS(HCO3-)/qP not computable without fabricating values.

up.method: Metabolite quantification (non-linear/sigmoidal fits to glucose consumption over a 96 h batch time course, Supplementary Figure S5B-E); used to define carbon-uptake model constraints (model units mmol x g(cells)^-1 x h^-1); numeric rate reported only in Supplementary figures/tables

sec.method: Metabolite quantification over 96 h batch time course (Supplementary Figure S5); lactic-acid production dominates during late growth; numeric rate in Supplement

Metabolite quantification over 96 h batch time course (Supplementary Figure S5E); acetic-acid production favored during early growth (4 ATP/glucose) then shifts to lactic acid; acetate-production constraint fitted to experimentally determined lactic:acetic ratio; numeric rate in Supplement

records.snip: Batch culture growth experiments on glucose lead to a measurable decrease in medium pH (pH 7.8-5.5 during a 4-day growth course) due to secretion of lactic and acetic acid (Yus et al, 2009). A shift from energetically favorable acetic acid
records.notes: Growth: in vivo doubling time during exponential phase reported as ~20 h ('values measured for M. pneumoniae (20 h; Figure 3A)'); mu computed mu=ln2/td=0.693/20=0.0347 /h (growth_method=calculated from measured doubling time). Model in-silico doubling times were 2.3-3.8 h but these are PREDICTED (not experimental) and excluded. Substrate uptake (glucose) and product secretion (lactate, acetate) were MEASURED experimentally over a 96 h batch time course and fitted with sigmoidal functions (Supplementary Figure S5B-E) to derive model constraints in units mmol x g(cells)^-1 x h^-1; however the explicit numeric rate values reside in the Supplementary material (not in the main text provided), so rate fields are null with is_experimental=true. Medium: rich Hayflick medium + 55 mM glucose (pH experiment) and a chemically defined medium (Yus et al 2009, Supplementary Table S5); full Hayflick base composition not enumerated in this article. Temperature not explicitly stated (M. pneumoniae standard is 37C but not written, left null). pH starts ~7.8 and falls to 5.5 over the 4-day batch. No experimental 13C-MFA net-flux numbers reported (13C labelling was used qualitatively to confirm glycolytic intermediate labelling; all quantitative fluxes are FBA model predictions), so mfa_fluxes empty. Strain not explicitly named in text; iJW145 model built on Yus et al 2009 which used strain M129.

records.strain: EMBL-1 (NCBI 16S accession MZ475068; 99.01% ANI to K. variicola RS_GCF_000828055.2)
records.med: Mineral/basal medium with PVC film as sole energy and organic carbon source (composition not stated in text)
records.cite: Zhe Zhang et al. Polyvinyl chloride degradation by a bacterium isolated from the gut of insect larvae. Nature Communications (2022). DOI:10.1038/s4146

up.method: gravimetric weight loss of film (not converted to a per-biomass molar rate)

records.snip: causing visible cracks on the surface of the PVC film, accompanied by an increase in the strain biomass concentration, i.e., OD600 from 0.20 to about 0.60. ... the weight loss of the film reached ~7% ... the OD600 increased from 0.20 to 0.5
records.notes: This is a plastic-biodegradation study, NOT a GEM/13C-MFA physiology paper; no substrate-uptake or product-secretion rates in mmol/gDW/h and no defined medium composition are reported. The ONLY quantitative prokaryote growth datum is an endpoint OD600 change on PVC film as sole carbon source: OD600 0.20 -> 0.58 over 10 days (also reported as 0.20 -> ~0.60 after 10 days in the initial screening; treated as the same condition). Calculated growth rate assuming OD proportional to biomass and exponential growth over the interval: mu = ln(X2/X1)/t = ln(0.58/0.20)/(10 d x 24 h/d) = ln(2.9)/240 h = 1.0647/240 = 0.00444 /h. Doubling time td = ln2/mu = 0.6931/0.00444 = 156 h. CAVEAT: two-point endpoint estimate over 10 days, almost certainly not true exponential growth, so mu is a crude lower-order approximation only. Film weight loss over 90 days reached 19.57% (gravimetric) and Mn/Mw decreased 12.4%/15.0% by APC, but these are not convertible to a per-biomass molar uptake rate from the numbers given. Temperature and pH of the degradation culture not stated in the read text; left null. GC-MS identified degradation products (e.g. adipic-acid esters) but these are film transformation products, not quantified microbial secretion rates. Record retained per exhaustiveness mandate but flagged low confidence; not suitable as a GEM constraint on its own.

records.notes: Resting washed-cell suspension assay (OD600=1.0), NOT a growth culture; OD 'virtually unchanged' after 16 h (Table S2) so no growth_rate/doubling_time derivable (mu~0). CH3SH secretion 477.71+/-26.30 ppm is a headspace GC value at 5 mM L-methionine, pH 6.5, 16 h (Table 1); a comparable ~500 ppm plateau at 3.5-5.0 mM Met at pH 6.5 is shown in Fig 2A. Methionine uptake reported only as ~50% of the supplied substrate over 12 h (Fig 3); the exact starting methionine concentration for the Fig 3 time-course is not explicitly stated (mCDM was supplemented with 0.5-5.0 mM Met 'as necessary'), so an absolute mmol or mmol/gDW/h uptake rate cannot be computed without assumption; no gDW-per-OD conversion is given, precluding biomass-specific normalization. 13C5,15N-methionine tracing (10 mM, 6 h) was performed but reported as relative isotopomer abundances (m+n) over time, not net fluxes with units, so mfa_fluxes left empty.
