records.snip: cocultures of F. nucleatum and S. gordonii WT exhibited the highest level of consumption of methionine ... release of 1.2 and 0.95 mM ornithine, respectively ... suggesting an uptake of 0.25 mM ornithine by F. nucleatum ... approximately 2.
records.notes: Coculture of two resting washed-cell suspensions (each OD600=1.0); no net growth reported (bacterial growth not significantly affected by ornithine addition, data not shown; OD unchanged). Net ornithine accumulation in coculture = 0.95 mM over 12 h -> 0.95/12 = 0.079 mM/h. Inferred F. nucleatum ornithine uptake = 1.2 mM (S. gordonii monoculture) - 0.95 mM (coculture) = 0.25 mM over 12 h, as stated verbatim by authors. CH3SH enhancement ~2.3-fold (0.5 mM Met) to ~3-fold (1 mM Met) vs monocultures; enhancement preserved in non-contact coculture (diffusible factor = ornithine). 13C5,15N-methionine intracellular tracing (CE-TOF-MS, 6 h) mapped fate of Met into methionine cycle and polyamine/salvage pathway but reported only relative isotopomer abundances (m+n), not net fluxes with units -> mfa_fluxes empty. Gene-expression: mgl/metK up 2.3-4.2-fold, metK/metH/metQ/metI up 4.0-5.6-fold in coculture (qPCR), consistent with enhanced Met uptake.

records.snip: The homoacetogenic Acetobacterium lineage dominating the H2-fed enrichment used 1.6 umol isoprene h-1 as an electron acceptor in addition to HCO3-. Growth of the homoacetogen with isoprene produces 40% less acetate than with H2 and HCO3- al
records.notes: Prokaryote = Acetobacterium wieringae (Gram-positive homoacetogen). This is a proteogenomics/comparative-genomics paper; no growth rate (mu), doubling time, or per-gDW metabolic rates are reported for the culture. Only quantitative physiology datum in-text is the isoprene consumption rate 1.6 umol/h, which is attributed to the H2-fed enrichment characterized in the group's PRIOR study (ref 23: Kronen et al. 2019, ISME J, doi:10.1038/s41396-018-0338-z), not newly measured here. Acetate 40% reduction is stated qualitatively (no absolute rate/yield). Cell-suspension conditions (30C, N2/5e4 Pa H2, 40 mM HCO3-, 1 mM isoprene) are from Fig. S1 legend. No arithmetic performed (no derivable numbers).

records.notes: Rate is SECONDHAND: measured on Acetobacterium wieringae strain Y in an independent study (ref 24: Jin et al. 2022, mBio, doi:10.1128/mbio.02086-22), merely quoted in this paper's introduction. Reported directly as 1.74 umol/h and 262.3 +/- 21.2 uM/day; no unit conversion or arithmetic needed. No growth rate, biomass, medium composition, temperature or pH given in this paper. Included for completeness and flagged as cited, not originally measured here.

records.snip: Hourly CH4 production rates across cultures of larger cyanobacteria were in the range of 0.1 to 3.4 μmol g−1 hour−1 in individual experiments and a mean of 0.51 ± 0.26 μmol g−1 hour−1.
records.notes: Aggregate secretion rate reported for the group of 'larger cyanobacteria' (per-strain values only in Fig. 3, not in text). CH4 secretion rate = 0.51 ± 0.26 µmol CH4 (g dry weight)^-1 h^-1, individual-experiment range 0.1-3.4 µmol g^-1 h^-1. No growth rate (mu) reported: study measured CH4 production, not growth. No pH given; media BG11/f/2/YBCII cited by reference, compositions not stated (BG11 P content noted as ca. 200 µmol/L in Discussion). Temperatures per Table 1: BG11 strains 30°C, Phormidium & Trichodesmium 26°C, Nodularia 20°C — group temperature set to the modal 30°C. Carbon source NaH13CO3 (13C-bicarbonate); inorganic-C uptake demonstrated by 13C incorporation into CH4 but not quantified as a rate.

sec.method: UPLC-APCI-HRMS/TLC; 0.9 mM total estrogen produced over 15 d (360 h); rate=0.9/360
UPLC-APCI-HRMS/TLC; 1.4 mM produced over 15 d (360 h); rate=1.4/360

Not directly quantified; the removed steroidal C-19 methyl group is inferred (transcriptomics/proteomics + 13C-testosterone tracing) to be fully oxidised to CO2 via the oxidative Wood-Ljungdahl pathway; no acetate formed (acetate kinase absent).

records.snip: After 15 d of anaerobic incubation, ~2.3 mM testosterone was consumed, yielding 0.9 mM estrogen and 1.4 mM androstandiol. During growth, strain TUW77 cell density increased from ~2.3 × 10^4 cells/mL to 1.3 × 10^7 cells/mL over 15 d, with a
records.notes: Anaerobic testosterone-fermentation condition. Growth: doubling time 39.2 h reported directly; mu calculated mu=ln2/td=0.693/39.2=0.0177 /h (growth_method=calculated). Rates are VOLUMETRIC (mmol/L/h) because biomass is given only as cells/mL (2.3e4->1.3e7 over 15 d), so gDW normalisation is impossible. Testosterone qS=2.3 mM/(15 d x 24 h=360 h)=0.0064 mmol/L/h. Estrogen (estrone+estradiol) q=0.9/360=0.0025; androstanediol q=1.4/360=0.0039 mmol/L/h. Product yields: estrogen/testosterone=0.9/2.3=0.39 mol/mol; androstanediol/testosterone=1.4/2.3=0.61 mol/mol (sum~1.0, consistent with testosterone fully partitioned to the two products). CO2 production inferred (oxidative WLP), not quantified. DCB-1 mineral medium composition is cited (ref 17), not stated in text -> medium_composition_stated=false; only testosterone (1 mM) given. Temp 30C, N2/CO2 80:20 headspace, dark. pH not stated. 13C-testosterone tracing done but no net MFA flux table reported.

Inferred full oxidation of the C-19 methyl group to CO2 via oxidative WLP with electrons routed to AQDS; not quantified

product_yields.note: Under AQDS, testosterone->estrogen only (no androstanediol); complete conversion, but no numeric mM/rate reported in main text

records.snip: the addition of anthraquinone-2,6-disulfonate, an extracellular electron acceptor, to testosterone-fed TUW77 cultures enabled complete testosterone conversion into estrogen without androstanediol accumulation (anaerobic testosterone oxidati
records.notes: Distinct condition = anaerobic testosterone oxidation with AQDS as terminal electron acceptor (vs the fermentation record above). Qualitatively: complete testosterone -> estrogen conversion, no androstanediol, 14 d incubation. No time-resolved concentrations, no growth rate, no doubling time reported for this specific condition in the main text, so all rate/growth fields null (values likely in SI Fig. 4). Included as a separate experimental condition per one-record-per-condition rule. Medium DCB-1 composition cited (ref 17) not stated; AQDS concentration not stated in text -> medium_composition_stated=false.

mfa.reaction_or_metabolite: flux through glutamine (net glutamine turnover, wild type, kinetic 15N-flux profiling)
mfa.note: measured by kinetic flux profiling with 15N-ammonium; intracellular concentration-based flux, not gDW-normalized exchange flux (Supplementary Table 1)

mfa.reaction_or_metabolite: glutamine feeding into GOGAT (wild type, derived)
mfa.note: derived by authors as 54 (WT glutamine flux) - 13 (GOGAT-independent flux measured in delta-GOGAT); paper states 41 mM/min glutamine feeds GOGAT in WT

mfa.reaction_or_metabolite: glutamate produced via GS/GOGAT cycle (wild type, derived)
mfa.note: authors: 41 mM/min glutamine into GOGAT x 1:2 glutamine:glutamate stoichiometry = 82 mM/min glutamate, >85% of total glutamate biosynthetic flux

mfa.reaction_or_metabolite: flux through glutamine in delta-GOGAT (gltD) mutant
mfa.note: GOGAT-independent residual glutamine flux measured in delta-GOGAT; reference point for the 41 mM/min derivation

records.snip: knocking out GOGAT substantially slowed assimilation of 15N-ammonium into glutamine, with flux through glutamine reduced from 54 mM/min to 13 mM/min... consistent with 41 mM/min of glutamine feeding into GOGAT in wild type, and generating 8
records.notes: Wild-type E. coli NCM 3722 grown on Gutnick minimal salts (cited Gutnick et al 1969, full salts composition NOT stated in text) + 10 mM NH4Cl + 0.4% glucose, 37C, filter culture on agarose. Doubling time 58 min reported (Fig 2A). growth_rate mu = ln2/td = 0.6931/(58/60 h) = 0.6931/0.9667 = 0.717 /h (calculated); doubling_time_h = 58/60 = 0.967 h. pH~7 stated later in text ('our experiments were conducted at pH~7'). No glucose uptake or by-product secretion rates measured, so uptake_rates/secretion_rates empty. mfa_fluxes are intracellular kinetic-flux-profiling fluxes in mM/min (glutamine/glutamate synthesis via GS/GOGAT), NOT gDW-normalized exchange fluxes; captured verbatim with arithmetic the authors give (54->13 mM/min on GOGAT KO; 54-13=41 mM/min into GOGAT; 41x2=82 mM/min glutamate).

records.cite: Jie Yuan et al. Metabolomics-driven quantitative analysis of ammonia assimilation in E. coli. Molecular Systems Biology (2009). DOI:10.1038/msb.2009.6
records.snip: growth of the culture did not stop, but continued at a reduced rate for ~1.5 additional doublings (doubling time ~220 min)... Consistent with the culture being nitrogen-limited, the GS protein content was elevated
records.notes: Same WT NCM 3722, filter culture, but 2 mM NH4Cl initial (N-limited). During limitation doubling time ~220 min reported (Fig 2A, cyan). mu = ln2/(220/60 h) = 0.6931/3.6667 = 0.189 /h (calculated); doubling_time_h = 220/60 = 3.667 h. Earlier in this run cells grew at the unlimited rate (~58 min doubling) until OD650~0.4 before N ran out; the reduced-rate 220-min value characterizes the N-limited steady phase. No exchange uptake/secretion rates measured.

records.cite: Jie Yuan et al. Metabolomics-driven quantitative analysis of ammonia assimilation in E. coli. Molecular Systems Biology (2009). DOI:10.1038/msb.2009.6
records.snip: The growth rate was partially restored (doubling time ~117 min) by transferring the ammonium-limited filter culture to plates with 10 mM ammonium (Figure 2A, blue dots)
records.notes: WT NCM 3722 filter culture, N-limited then upshifted to 10 mM NH4Cl. Post-upshift doubling time ~117 min (Fig 2A, blue). mu = ln2/(117/60 h) = 0.6931/1.95 = 0.355 /h (calculated); doubling_time_h = 117/60 = 1.95 h. Growth only partially restored vs the 58-min N-replete rate because cells previously experienced N-limitation (reduced max growth rate, tau0 = 90 min in model). No exchange rates measured.

records.notes: Environmental diazotrophic Fe(III)-reducing bacterium; rates are VOLUMETRIC (mM or µM), not biomass-normalized (no gDW reported). Fe(III) reduction rate computed = 5 mM / 7 days = 0.714 mM/day = 0.0298 mM/h (nearly-complete reduction of 5 mM). Nitrogenase activity 695.4±37.9 µM ethylene at 3 d (Fig 2A); fixed 15N 140.2±3.4 µM at 3 d (Fig 2B). N2-fixation (vs Ar) raised max Fe(III) reduction rate by 771.3% and OD600 by 200%. Total amino-acid (N-fixing product) ~1.5x higher with Fe(III). No mu/doubling reported.

sec.method: acetylene reduction assay; ~614.6 µM acetylene reduced

records.snip: After 48 h of incubation, strain PCA reduced ~614.6 µM of acetylene (Fig 2C) and 59.8 µM of 15N2 (Fig 2D) through MNF... MNF (N2 group) significantly promoted the growth (OD600 increased by 344.4%) and DIR (maximum rate increased by 169.02%
records.notes: Model dissimilatory Fe(III)-reducing diazotroph. Acetate is the electron donor and carbon source (20 mM); Fe(III)-citrate the electron acceptor. Nitrogenase activity of PCA only observed when Fe(III)-citrate added. Nitrogenase ~614.6 µM ethylene and 59.8 µM 15N2 fixed at 48 h. N2 fixation raised OD600 by 344.4% and max Fe(III)-reduction rate by 169.02% (i.e. ~2.69-fold heterotrophic Fe-reduction enhancement). Fe(III) reduction rate estimated 5 mM/7 d = 0.714 mM/day (volumetric, not per gDW). pH of FWAFC not stated in main text (Supplementary S2). ATCC 51573 = G. sulfurreducens PCA.

records.snip: Compared to monocultures, where S. oneidensis MR-1 and A. humicireducens SgZ-5T achieved only 30% and 14% Fe(III) reduction, respectively, the co-culture exhibited markedly enhanced performance, achieving complete reduction of 5 mM Fe(III)
records.notes: Interspecies syntrophy: diazotroph (SgZ-5T) supplies N enabling DIRB (MR-1) Fe(III) reduction. Co-culture Fe(III) reduction rate = 5 mM / 7 d = 0.714 mM/day (volumetric). Monoculture controls in same medium: MR-1 30% (=1.5 mM, ~0.214 mM/day) and SgZ-5T 14% (=0.7 mM, ~0.10 mM/day) of 5 mM over the incubation. No nitrogenase activity in MR-1 monoculture (Fig S5); co-culture N-fixation enhanced only when Fe(III) present (no enhancement without Fe, Fig S6). No mu/gDW reported.

records.notes: No measured substrate-uptake or product-secretion rates in this mutation-accumulation paper; growth-parameter figures (Fig 2) give OD-based lag/rate/max but no numeric values in text. records.snip: The cell-free 3-keto-trehalose-containing culture supernatant used for fitness and enzymatic assays was obtained after 29 h of growth and contained 17.4 mM of trehalose and 7.9 mM 3-keto-trehalose.
records.notes: Incidental biotransformation measurement (not a dedicated growth-physiology study); no biomass reported so only VOLUMETRIC rates derivable, not per-gDW. Initial trehalose = 2% (w/v) = 20 g/L; using anhydrous MW 342.3 g/mol -> 20/342.3 = 58.4 mM (if dihydrate MW 378.3 -> 52.9 mM). Consumed = 58.4 - 17.4 = 41.0 mM over 29 h -> 41.0/29 = 1.41 mM/h. 3-keto-trehalose produced 7.9 mM / 29 h = 0.272 mM/h. Product/consumed yield = 7.9/41.0 = 0.19 mol/mol (remainder consumed for growth/oxidation). A. rhizogenes (alphaproteobacterium) periplasmically oxidizes trehalose to 3-keto-trehalose. Organism/condition is peripheral to the paper (which is a Bacteroides thetaiotaomicron VPI-5482 Tn-seq gene-fitness study; Bt itself reports only qualitative OD/fitness growth, no numeric mu or uptake/secretion rates, hence no Bt record).

records.snip: Of the 2 g/L chitin that were completely consumed (with residual concentrations below the detection limit of 62 mg/L), 33%-36% of the input chitin carbon was recovered in biomass, 4.1%-4.5% in EPS, and 11%-14% in LMW metabolites, while the
records.notes: Batch bicarbonate-buffered bioreactor, Vibrio sp. 1A01 on 2 g/L colloidal chitin as sole C source, 27C, pH 7.8, early stationary phase. Values are a CLOSED CARBON BUDGET from elemental analysis of two replicate bioreactors, NOT time-resolved rates: biomass 33-36%, EPS 4.1-4.5%, LMW 11-14%, CO2 45-52% (by difference, sum=100%) of the input chitin carbon. EPS yield also expressed gravimetrically as ~45 mg dried EPS per g chitin consumed (Fig 1B); two other chitin degraders gave similar 4-7% EPS yields (figure-only, not tabulated). No specific growth rate (mu), doubling time, dilution rate, or per-gDW rates are reported, so mu cannot be derived (only endpoint OD600 to stationary was tracked; time not given). No qS in mmol/gDW/h derivable without biomass concentration and duration. EPS composition: proteins 13-23% g/g, polysaccharides 9-36% of C (glucose ~55% of monomers), DNA ~0.17% g/g. Medium = MBL (K2HPO4 1 mM, Na2SO4 1 mM; 4x seawater salts giving final NaCl 20, MgCl2*6H2O 3, CaCl2*2H2O 0.15, KCl 0.5 g/L; trace minerals + vitamins) with 50 mM NaHCO3 replacing the 50 mM TES buffer used in the standard MBL recipe. Nitrogen for the bioreactor run is supplied by the chitin (GlcNAc) itself; separate yield/EPS-consumption assays added 1 mM NH4Cl. This is a genome-scale-model-relevant carbon-allocation dataset (biomass/EPS/LMW/CO2 split) for the marine chitin degrader Vibrio sp. 1A01, a model organism for marine carbon cycling.

All is_experimental=false. temperature/pH not applicable (in-planta model). culture_mode left blank: none of the allowed enum values (batch/fed-batch/chemostat/turbidostat/retentostat/single-cell/co-culture) applies to an in-planta symbiotic bacteroid FBA simulation.

records.snip: The cultures were sampled (500 ul) every hour for OD600nm measurements and the culture supernatants ... from early (13 h) stationary phase were analysed by UPLC-MS for aromatic amino metabolites and by GC-MS for lactate. ... Curves show mea
records.notes: This is NOT a genome-scale-model / 13C-MFA paper; it is a Bifidobacterium physiology/enzymology study. The only extractable-in-text quantitative metabolic data are in-vitro ENZYME kinetics for the type-4 LDH/ALDH (kcat/K0.5: indolepyruvate 194 s-1 mM-1, 4-hydroxyphenylpyruvate 16 s-1 mM-1, phenylpyruvate 11 s-1 mM-1; Fig. 3) - these are purified-enzyme parameters, NOT whole-cell growth-condition uptake/secretion fluxes, so not entered as rates. The whole-cell growth physiology that IS reported (specific growth / doubling times in Fig. 2d; secreted ILA, PLA, 4-OH-PLA and lactate amounts in Fig. 2e; production by ~14 type strains on glucose vs HMO in Fig. 2a) has its NUMERIC values only in figure images / Source data files, which are not present in the PMC XML fulltext. Therefore all rate fields are null (no numbers invented, per instruction). One representative record emitted for the genetically defined B. longum ssp. longum 105-A WT batch condition to preserve organism+medium+condition and the qualitative measured secretion. Medium = MRSc = MRS broth + 2% (w/v) glucose + 0.05% (w/v) L-cysteine (base MRS component list not itemised in paper). Conditions: 37 C, anaerobic, batch, shaking; supernatant at 13 h early stationary phase. No pH, no growth rate value, no yields extractable from text. Growth was not impaired by type-4 ldh disruption (Fig. 2d).

records.notes: GEOCHEMICAL/AUTOTROPHIC REDOX system, not classical heterotrophic GEM physiology; included as a defined pure-culture prokaryote growth condition with measured substrate transformation. Growth_rate CALCULATED from explicit Nao1 (no-IPTG) cell counts: mu = ln(4.20e5/1.04e5)/(8 d x 24 h) = ln(4.038)/192 = 1.396/192 = 0.00727 /h; doubling_time = ln2/mu = 0.6931/0.00727 = 95.3 h. Full MSM composition is in Supplementary Information (not in main text) -> medium_composition_stated:false; only amended components listed. MIC of Sb(III) for R. palustris in MSM = 0.5 mM; 0.1 mM had no metabolic effect (why 0.1 mM chosen). No biomass in gDW -> rates volumetric only, cannot convert to mmol/gDW/h. Carbon fixed from 13C-bicarbonate (autotrophy) confirmed by IRMS + nanoSIMS but CO2 uptake rate not reported. records.notes: MIXED-COMMUNITY soil enrichment microcosm (NOT a pure prokaryote culture); functional organism inferred as uncultured Symbiobacteriaceae sp. by 16S + metagenomic binning. Included because substrate consumption and product formation are fully quantified and time-resolved for a prokaryotic redox metabolism. All rates are VOLUMETRIC (per L culture) computed over the 8-day incubation (192 h); no biomass measured -> cannot normalize to mmol/gDW/h. Arithmetic: Sb(III) uptake = (1.03-0.46) mM / 192 h = 0.57/192 = 0.00297 mmol/L/h (=0.0713 mM/day); note ~10% (57.45 uM) of Sb(III) oxidation was ABIOTIC via labile Fe(III), so net microbial slightly lower. Nitrate uptake = (9.97-6.06)/192 = 3.91/192 = 0.02036 mmol/L/h (=0.489 mM/day). Sb(V) secretion = 0.51/192 = 0.00266 mmol/L/h (=0.0638 mM/day). Nitrite secretion = 2.69/192 = 0.01401 mmol/L/h (=0.336 mM/day). N2O ~17% and 15N2 ~3% of NO3--reduced products (Fig 1E), reported as percentages only. Full MSM composition in Supplementary Information -> medium_composition_stated:false. Nitrate consumption is partly by non-Sb electron donors (organic matter, Fe(II)); a significant negative correlation between Sb(III) oxidation and nitrate reduction (P=0.0003) supports coupling.

Doubling time calc: ln2/0.156=4.44 h. qCH4 derived approx from mu/Y=0.156/0.97=0.1608 gCH4/gDW/h=10.0 mmol/gDW/h (mu_max and yield not necessarily co-temporal, so approximate). pH controlled 7-8 (Methods) / 7-7.3 (Results text); 30±0.1 C. Productivity 147.2 mg cell protein/L/h.
records.method: reported (mu_max 0.156 h-1 from Supplementary Table 2); doubling time calculated td=ln2/mu=0.6931/0.156=4.44 h

records.snip: For T. saccharolyticum, the final ethanol titer reached 949 ± 6 mM (43.7 ± 0.3 g/L), with 203 ± 2 mM (36.5 ± 0.4 g/L) of glucose accumulating in the medium. Although cellobiose was fully consumed by ~90 h, fermentation was not substrate-lim
records.notes: Primary detailed fermentation (Fig 2). Cellobiose (MW 342.3) 140 g/L = 409 mM, fully consumed by ~90 h. Cellobiose gives 2 glucose; glucose-equivalents = 140 × (2×180.16/342.30) = 147.4 g/L (818 mM). Residual free glucose accumulated in medium = 36.5 g/L (203 mM), so net glucose-equiv consumed = 147.4 - 36.5 = 110.9 g/L (615 mM). Ethanol yield = 43.7/110.9 = 0.394 g/g; molar 949/615 = 1.54 mol/mol = 77% of 2.0. Substrate is cellobiose but the organism excretes free glucose (listed under secretion). No gDW/specific-rate data reported.
records.method: not reported (time-course only; no numeric mu)

records.snip: For Z. mobilis fermentation, the final ethanol titer reached 1553 ± 15 mM (71.6 ± 0.7 g/L), with 174 ± 2 mM (31.3 ± 0.4 g/L) glucose still present at the end of fermentation.
records.notes: Primary detailed fermentation (Fig 3). Glucose 160 g/L = 888 mM initial; residual 31.3 g/L (174±2 mM); consumed = 128.7 g/L (714 mM). Ethanol final 71.6 g/L (1553 mM) but 10 g/L (~217 mM) was added at start, so net produced = 61.6 g/L (1336 mM). Yield = 61.6/128.7 = 0.479 g/g; molar 1336/714 = 1.87 mol/mol = 94% of theoretical 2.0. Z. mobilis uses Entner-Doudoroff pathway. No pyruvate accumulation observed; authors infer titer limited by substrate uptake not central metabolism. No gDW/specific-rate data.

records.notes: Only quantitative growth parameter in the paper. ~50-fold increase in viable CFU over 6 h. Paper states doubling time ~1 h. Cross-check: mu = ln(50)/6 h = 3.912/6 = 0.652 /h; doubling time = ln2/0.652 = 1.06 h (~1 h, consistent with authors). growth_rate_per_h reported here as 0.65 (calculated from 50-fold/6 h); doubling_time 1.0 h as stated. up.method: short-term [14C]bicarbonate primary production (net and gross primary production); values plotted in Fig. 1 over the diel cycle but no single numeric rate given in the main text

yields.elemental_composition: Particulate C/P molar ratio = 121:1 +/- 17:1 under balanced growth (molar NH4+/H2PO4- = 16:1); rose to 464:1 +/- 28:1 under P-limitation (molar NH4+/H2PO4- = 800:1). Cited to reference 14 (Bertilsson et al.), not measured in this study.

records.snip: By simulating their growth conditions over a diel cycle, we calculated an optimal growth rate of 0.62 day-1, while the experimental growth rates were 0.62 +/- 0.04 day-1 (mean +/- standard deviation). ... 14-h/10-h light/dark cycle at 20 to
records.notes: EXPERIMENTAL growth rate = 0.62 +/- 0.04 day-1 for P. marinus MED4 on PRO99, 14h/10h light/dark, 20-24 C. This value is CITED FROM Zinser et al. (reference 26 in this paper; the most comprehensive data set relating carbon fixation and photophysiology to growth), and the iJC568 model reproduced it (computed 0.62 day-1). Conversions: mu = 0.62 day-1 / 24 h/day = 0.02583 h-1 (rounded 0.0258). Doubling time td = ln2/mu = 0.6931/0.62 = 1.118 day = 1.118 x 24 = 26.8 h. This study's own axenic MED4 culture was grown at 24 C, peak irradiance 45 umol photons m-2 s-1 (Materials and Methods); P-limited variant used 2 uM H2PO4- giving N/P ratio 200, but no growth rate was reported for the P-limited condition so it is not emitted as a separate record. Organism is an obligate photoautotroph, so there is no organic substrate uptake rate; the only measured flux is [14C]bicarbonate primary production, reported graphically (Fig. 1) with no single numeric value in text. iJC568 is a constraint-based FBA reconstruction (not a 13C-MFA study), so internal fluxes are model-computed, not experimental; mfa_fluxes left empty. Full PRO99 composition not given in this paper (cited to ref 31).

records.snip: R. pomeroyi growth rate in the model systems (0.95 d-1 for the first 4 d of the study and 0.31 d-1 for the 8 d average) fell within the range reported for Rhodobacteraceae in natural marine systems
records.notes: Experimental growth rate of the prokaryote R. pomeroyi DSS-3 in a diatom (T. pseudonana) coculture, measured by flow-cytometry cell counts (cells mL^-1) with CFU-based partitioning of R. pomeroyi within the community over an 8-d experiment. Paper reports two windows: mu1=0.95 d^-1 (first 4 d, exponential) and mu2=0.31 d^-1 (8-d average). Conversions: mu1=0.95/24=0.0396 h^-1; doubling time td=ln2/mu1=0.6931/0.0396=17.5 h (=0.6931/0.95=0.730 d). mu2=0.31/24=0.0129 h^-1; td=ln2/mu2=0.6931/0.0129=53.7 h (=2.24 d). Primary record uses the first-4-d exponential rate. No substrate uptake or product secretion rates in mmol/gDW/h are reported; substrate/N-utilization tests (glucose 5 mM +/- ammonium/nitrate 1 mM; Marine Basal Medium substrate tests with glycolate/DMSP/glutamate/glutamine/asparagine/aspartate at 12 mM carbon) report only growth yes/no, not rates. Medium is L1+Si ASW (cited from ref 78, Guillard/Hargraves L1); full component concentrations not given in-text, so composition left empty. pH not stated. Precultures: YTSS (4 g/L yeast extract, 2.5 g/L tryptone, 20 g/L sea salts) +/- 40 mM sucrose or glucose (1 mM)+ammonium (0.5 mM) in L1 ASW - these are inoculum/preparation media, not the growth-rate condition.
records.method: reported (mu reported as 0.95 d^-1 for first 4 d; converted to per-h and doubling time calculated)

records.snip: predicted C4-dicarboxylate import by N2-fixing bacteroids (1.3 mmol h-1 [g bacteroid dry weight]-1) was similar to experimentally determined uptake rates by S. meliloti bacteroids (1.1 to 1.3 mmol h-1 [g bacteroid dry weight]-1)
records.notes: This is a genome-scale metabolic reconstruction/FBA paper (ViNE, S. meliloti nodulating M. truncatula); virtually all quantitative rates are FBA-PREDICTED, not measured. The ONLY experimental prokaryote rate mentioned is the C4-dicarboxylate uptake of 1.1-1.3 mmol/gDW/h by S. meliloti bacteroids, and it is CITED from prior literature (references 35 = Zhao H et al.; 91 = McRae DG, Miller RW, Berndt WB, Joy K, 'Transport of C4-dicarboxylates...'), NOT generated in this study. Recorded here only because the task asks to capture cited experimental rates and flag the citation. No defined medium, temperature, pH, or growth rate is given for this bacteroid condition. Other numbers in the paper are model outputs and were deliberately NOT recorded as experimental: FBA specific-growth-rate reductions of ~0.043 h-1 (glucose) and ~0.030 h-1 (succinate) from adding NGAM (predicted); FBA plant growth ~0.044 g/day/gDW and N2-fixation ~3 umol/h/g plant DW (predicted); N2-fixation efficiency constant 150 umol/h/g nodule DW (model parameter). Cited plant (Medicago sativa) values (growth ~0.1 g/day/gDW; N2-fixation ~7 umol/h/g by acetylene reduction; carbon cost 3-5 g C/g N) are for the PLANT, not a prokaryote, and are cited, so excluded.

records.notes: Prokaryote (archaeon) pure-culture experimental substrate-uptake condition: ammonia oxidation = NH3 consumption -> NO2- production, measured by 15N-NH4+ tracer accumulation in NOx-. Numeric rate values are NOT in the main-text XML: volumetric AOR for SCM1 appear only in Fig. 1m, and the fitted kinetic parameters Vmax(NH3), Km(NH3) and affinity alpha(NH3) are only in Supplementary Tables 4-5 and Fig. 2 (0 inline <table> elements in this XML) -> rate fields left null rather than invented. Only text-stated quantitative anchors: overall across-study AOR range 0.9-376 nM d-1 (spans ALL field stations + culture, NOT SCM1-specific, so not assigned here); per-0.1-unit-pH-drop AOA pure-culture responses Km(NH3) -19%+/-7%, alpha(NH3) +94%+/-7%, Vmax -9.0%+/-0.1% (these are normalized acidification sensitivities, not absolute rates). Medium: SCM composition not given in this paper (cited to refs 11,41); marine autotroph, HEPES-buffered, NH4+ as sole energy substrate, CO2/bicarbonate as C source. pH: control plus an acidified gradient (values not stated numerically in main text). No biomass, growth rate, doubling time or yields reported -> qS in mmol/gDW/h not derivable. Volumetric-only, not biomass-normalized. Other three papers in this batch yielded no records: PMC12929690 (host macrophage immunometabolism, U-13C-glucose tracing on mouse cells not the bacterium P. aeruginosa PAO1); PMC12932662 (mouse diet/gut-microbiota behaviour; B. longum APC1472 only grown to OD600 1.1-1.2 ~1e9 CFU/ml as inoculum, no rates); PMC12949522 (FEMS Microbiology Reviews review article, no primary data).

records.snip: Complete utilization of taurine as electron acceptor in modified Desulfovibrio liquid medium with electron donors lactate and pyruvate in excess resulted in production of nearly quantitative amounts of H2S and excess acetate (Fig. 1d).
records.notes: Genuine experimental anaerobic prokaryote growth condition (taurine respiration), but NO numeric specific rates, growth rate (mu), doubling time, or biomass (gDW) are stated in the article text. The time-course of taurine, sulfide and acetate is shown only graphically in Fig. 1d, with underlying numbers in a Source Data file not accessible to this curation. No rate could be computed without inventing numbers, so all rate fields are null. Qualitative experimental facts captured: taurine (10 mmol/l) completely consumed as electron acceptor; near-stoichiometric H2S production; excess acetate produced; NH3 released but not measured; pyruvate not measured. Additional stated (qualitative) physiology, not turned into records for lack of numbers: growth rate and final yield increased with taurine at 20 and 40 mmol/l vs 10 mmol/l, inhibited at >=60 mmol/l; lower rate/yield when pyruvate omitted; formate also usable as e-donor (extended lag, lower yield); 3-sulfolactate and thiosulfate usable as alternative e-acceptors (10 mmol/l, with lactate+pyruvate); no growth on DHPS, isethionate, cysteate, sulfate, or sulfite; grew equally at pH 6-8.5, optimum temperature 27-32 C with reduced yield at 37 and 42 C; acetate (20 mmol/l) tested as carbon source with H2. Other three papers in this batch (PMC10505187 CRISPRi promoter-response study in E. coli across minimal glucose/minimal glycerol/LB, PMC10516654 MrpR structural biology in B. subtilis, PMC10523453 in-vivo CRISPR knockdown of gut Bacteroides in gnotobiotic mice) report NO experimental uptake/secretion/growth rates and yield zero records.

N. inopinata ENR4, N. europaea ATCC 19718) were also grown batch in MWM, but the main text gives only RELATIVE growth retention (e.g. 7.9%, 22.5%, 81.9%, 80.7% of 10x-MWM rate), no absolute mu -> not separately recorded.

records.notes: NON-STANDARD growth condition: this is a photoelectrocatalytic-microbial biohybrid (electrode-immobilized cells, external electrons, CO2 co-substrate), not a conventional planktonic fermentation. Succinate production rate 1.41 g/L/h/cm2 is normalized to electrode area, NOT biomass (no gDW / OD / growth rate reported) -> cannot convert to mmol/gDW/h. Product yield = 0.78 g succinic acid / g glucose (biohybrid). Succinic acid = 57.18% of total product (Fig 3f). Byproducts acetic/formic/pyruvic acid detected but not quantified in text. Comparators: EC-biohybrid 0.75, PC-biohybrid 0.58, dark/open-circuit 0.21 g/L/h/cm2. Cells (WT and Au@A.succinogenes) resuspended to OD600 ~2.0 for immobilization. Strain 130Z ATCC 55618. Fermentation at 37 C, anaerobic, CO2-saturated.

records.snip: The culture consistently had a 30-60-day lag phase and required more than 3 months to reach full growth: around 10^5 16S rRNA gene copies ml-1. The doubling time was estimated to be approximately 14-25 days. ... cultivations were performed
records.notes: Prokaryote = archaeon (Asgard/Lokiarchaeota), qualifies for GrowthDB. Growth quantified only by qPCR of 16S rRNA gene copies (not OD or gDW), so NO uptake/secretion rate is expressible in mmol/gDW/h and none are derivable (no biomass mass, no substrate delta with time given numerically). Growth rate CALCULATED from reported doubling time: mu = ln2/td. td = 14 days = 336 h -> mu = 0.693/336 = 0.00206 /h; td = 25 days = 600 h -> mu = 0.693/600 = 0.001155 /h; hence mu approx 0.00116-0.00206 /h. Doubling time (14-25 d) reported directly, computed by authors from semi-log plot of qPCR data. Medium/conditions from Methods: 20 C, pH 7.5 (adjusted at 25 C), anaerobic N2/CO2 80:20, static (no shaking), dark; inoculum 20% v/v pure co-culture with Methanogenium. Obligate syntroph: growth depends on H2/formate-scavenging partner (Methanogenium or Methanobacterium sp. MO-MB1; SRB Halodesulfovibrio in tri-culture); 10 mM molybdate and 2-bromoethanesulfonate suppressed growth. Substrates catabolized = amino acids (ten catabolized via 2-oxoacid intermediates); products for interspecies transfer = H2, formate, CO2 (partner converts to CH4). Amino acid uptake experimentally verified by depletion monitoring (GC) and by 13C/15N SIP-NanoSIMS. Other tested substrate conditions (casamino acids alone; peptone + powdered milk; 10x and 100x diluted CA+20AA+PM) and temperature series (4-20 C+) did NOT significantly change growth rate/yield (data not shown / reported as no improvement), so a single representative optimal-condition record is emitted. No 13C-MFA flux map, no biomass-specific fluxes, no yields reported.
records.method: reported (doubling time 14-25 days from semi-log plot of qPCR 16S rRNA copies); mu calculated from doubling time

records.snip: The A. fermentans P41 was cultured in PYG liquid medium for 48 h, followed by acid production detection and untargeted metabolomic analysis. The concentration of volatile fatty acids (VFAs) was measured using gas chromatography... Subsequen
records.notes: Only genuinely experimental prokaryote pure-culture condition in this batch. A. fermentans P41 grown in PYG liquid medium 48 h; VFA measured by GC confirming acetate + butyrate secretion (products qualitatively identified; numeric VFA concentrations reside in Supplementary Fig. S17, NOT in the accessible fulltext, so rate=null, no derivation possible). Medium composition of PYG not given numerically (peptone + yeast extract + glucose only). Culture temperature for the PYG liquid step is not explicitly stated in text; the anaerobic workstation used for isolation/colony purification was held at 37 C, and the separate mixed-community in-vitro rumen fermentation (Menke medium + rumen fluid + substrate) ran at 39 C for 48 h. NOTE: the paper's 'growth rate' language refers to calf average daily gain (host animal), NOT microbial specific growth rate; no mu, no OD, no doubling time for the microbe. The in-vitro fermentation that reported increased butyrate proportion (P<0.05) with P41 addition is a MIXED rumen-fluid community assay reporting molar proportions, not a per-strain rate, so it is not recorded as a separate quantitative record. Other three papers in batch reported NO experimental prokaryote rates: PMC11260450 (RhlE RNA helicase, P. aeruginosa; LB, 37 C, qualitative cold-growth phenotype only); PMC11265412 (Lactobacillaceae PanGEM; all uptake/secretion fluxes FBA/FVA-PREDICTED; the sole experimental glucose uptake used as a lower bound is cited from Elliman-Duplat et al. / L. reuteri JCM 1112 reconstruction, ref 11 = doi:10.1186/s12934-019-1229-3, with no numeric value in main text); PMC11271444 (Salmonella Typhimurium; 'flux'=efflux pump, '13C' incidental, 'secretion'=T3SS).

records.snip: we estimated that the experimentally observed X. fastidiosa growth rate (0.1608 day-1) (19) was recovered in silico with a 3.37-fold reduction of the optimal FBPase flux ... This reduction converted the generation time from 1.45 h (typical
records.notes: This is primarily a genome-scale metabolic modeling (FBA) paper on the fastidious plant pathogen Xylella fastidiosa (model strain subsp. multiplex CFBP 8418). The ONLY quantitative experimental prokaryote growth-physiology value is a growth rate of 0.1608 day^-1, explicitly stated as 'experimentally observed' but CITED FROM reference 19 (Leite B, Andersen PC, Ishida ML) and NOT measured in this study. Conversion: mu = 0.1608 day^-1 / 24 h/day = 0.0067 h^-1; doubling time td = ln2/mu = 0.6931/0.0067 = 103.4 h, consistent with the paper's stated generation time of 103 h (fastidious). No experimental substrate uptake or product secretion rates are measured in this paper. The glutamine uptake of 1 mmol/gDW/h is an ARBITRARY generic modeling constraint (stated in Methods: 'a generic L-glutamine uptake rate of 1 mmol.h-1.g(DW)-1 was used' to avoid bias), NOT experimental -> is_experimental:false. The mfa_fluxes listed are FBA-PREDICTED carbon splits (95%/22%/34% of carbon uptake for glutamine->glutamate, oxaloacetate decarboxylase, malic enzyme respectively) from the Fig 1C simulation whose flux inputs/outputs were 'calculated from previous publications' (refs 19, 82: Leite et al.; Navarrete F, De La Fuente L), so they are NOT measured fluxes. In-paper experimental work was qualitative Biolog Phenotype MicroArray carbon-source utilization (Table 2), scored via respiration rate with a 0.125 h^-1 threshold (relatively fast '++' vs slow '+'); no per-substrate numeric respiration values are in the article XML (they reside in supplemental Data Set S3). Reported: generation time can vary 10-100 h across strains; ~100 h doubling for some strains. EPS production predicted to cost >25% of growth rate (comparison to R. solanacearum). Recorded as one condition despite weak experimental content because the growth rate is the only extractable experimental physiology datum.
records.method: reported (cited from ref 19, Leite et al.), converted day^-1 -> h^-1

records.snip: The final extracellular gluconate concentration was 11.5 mM, representing 18% of the glucose consumed ... ethanol ... accounted for ... only 35% for aerobic cultures ... 42% of the glucose consumed by aerobically grown cultures did not corr
records.notes: AEROBIC condition (O2 shift), Z. mobilis ZM4, 30C, minimal media, glucose sole C, batch. Oxygen inhibited both growth and ethanol production; growth 'markedly lower' under aerobic than anaerobic but NO numeric aerobic mu/doubling reported for the 24-h course, so growth_rate/doubling left null (growth_method reported/qualitative). Carbon utilization fractions (Fig 8C, NMR + LC-MS): ethanol 35% of glucose consumed => yield 0.35 x 2 = 0.70 mol/mol; gluconate 18% => 0.18 mol/mol, final 11.5 mM; 42% of glucose unaccounted (CO2 not measured). Gluconate excreted continuously over 24 h (redox balancing via glucose->gluconate diverting electrons to ETC). Acetate and pyruvate increased under aerobic (not %-quantified). Extracellular MEcDP transiently 2 uM by 30 min. Absolute rates in mmol/gDW/h NOT derivable (no gDW biomass, only OD600 and mM). 13C used qualitatively to trace label incorporation, not a quantitative net-flux 13C-MFA.

SECRETION: SCFA composition of C. acnes conditioned medium measured by GC-MS (Fig. 3D/E); the reconstitution mix reflecting that measured composition = 8 mM propionic, 0.6 mM acetic, 0.01 mM isovaleric, 0.02 mM butyric acid, so these are recorded as the measured secreted SCFA pool (concentrations, not biomass-normalized). No substrate-uptake rate quantified (C. acnes consumes TAGs -> SCFAs but not measured). No growth rate reported numerically (growth curves shown only graphically in Fig. 3F). pH left null: authors adjusted CM to pH 7 before cell assays, not stated as growth pH.

records.cite: Zhang, Liyu et al. A highly conserved core bacterial microbiota with nitrogen-fixation capacity inhabits the xylem sap in maize plants. Nature Communi

sec.compound: ethylene (C2H4, acetylene-reduction proxy for N2 fixation / nitrogenase activity)

records.snip: one strain mapping to the most abundant core OTU (29.2%) was identified as K. variicola and contained the entire nif gene cluster with nitrogenase activity of up to 1180 nmol C2H4 (mg protein hr)-1
records.notes: This is the ONLY experimental prokaryote metabolic rate found across the 4 papers in this batch. It is a nitrogenase (acetylene-reduction) enzyme activity, NOT a growth-coupled substrate-uptake or product-secretion exchange flux, and NOT expressed per gDW; captured for completeness and clearly flagged. Value 1180 nmol C2H4/mg protein/h is the maximum reported for K. variicola MNAZ1050 (Discussion; Fig 4d). No growth rate (mu), doubling time, temperature, pH, or medium composition is stated for this isolate. A second N-fixing core strain, Citrobacter sp. MNAZ1397, also showed high nitrogenase activity (Fig 4d) but no numeric value is given in text. Combinations (KC + helper strains) raised total nitrogenase activity above diazotrophs alone (Fig 4e) but only relative ratios, not absolute rates, are reported. In-planta SynCom (K. variicola MNAZ1050 + Citrobacter MNAZ1397 + Acinetobacter ACZLY512 + Rosenbergiella epipactidis YCCK550) contributed via BNF 4.01% (root), 11.81% (stem), 0.46% (leaf) of accumulated N by 15N isotopic dilution (Table 1: N2-fixed 2.55, 9.72, 0.63 mg respectively) — a whole-plant contribution, not a per-cell rate. No qS/Yxs derivable. PMC9159947 (Mtb CRISPRi drug-potency screen) and PMC9184479 (L. rhamnosus-C. albicans-IEC, in silico FBA/FVA only, growth by CFU with no reported rates) yielded NO experimental prokaryote uptake/secretion/growth rates.

Measured here: ammonium-N 2.5 (29% higher than daytime 1.8, i.e. ammonium assimilation ~light-independent/higher at night); urea-N assimilation 0.98 (urea assimilation ~5.5x lower than day, consistent with photosynthesis-powered urea assimilation); urea-N consumption 5.5 (similar to daytime 6.5, so urea is consumed day AND night but assimilated mainly by day). Only quantitative rate present: glucose maximum uptake rate = 6.1 mmol/g/h (i.e., 6.1 mmol gDW^-1 h^-1) applied as the FBA uptake upper bound for model wild_type_B6-2 -- this value is CITED from P. putida KT2440 (reference 54), NOT experimentally measured for strain B6-2 here, so is_experimental=false. An in vitro specific growth rate (MGR) of P. putida B6-2 on glucose WAS experimentally measured (regression of a growth curve on MSM + 2 g/L glucose) and used to validate the GEM: text states 'the difference between the in silico prediction and the in vitro measurements was lower than 20%' (Fig. 2b), but the numeric measured growth-rate value is only in the Fig. 2b image / Tables S4-S6 and is NOT in the extractable text, so growth_rate_per_h left null (no invented number). GEM-predicted MGR improvements of streamlined strain BGR4 over B6-2 were 1.15%-4.46% (predicted, not measured). Growth on 10 g/L glycerol and 2 mM phenol reported only qualitatively (BGR4 showed ~12 h shorter lag and 0.7-fold higher max OD600 on glycerol; ~24 h shorter lag and 1.1-fold higher max phenol degradation rate with dmpKLMNOP), with no numeric mu or gDW-normalized rates. Temperature/pH/rpm not stated in extractable text. Other three papers in this batch report no prokaryote physiological rates.

records.notes: Archaeal (prokaryote) enrichment co-culture; anaerobic butane oxidation coupled to sulfate reduction at 50 C (stated optimum growth temperature of Ca. S. butanivorans). Measured (experimental) quantities: butane supplied 6 mmol/L with >98% consumed by ~120 d; sulfate 28 mmol/L (also 5 mmol/L assays); excess-butane (30 mmol/L) assays reduced sulfate until ~20 mmol/L sulfide accumulated. DERIVED volumetric butane rate = 6 mmol/L / 120 d = 0.05 mmol/L/d (arithmetic shown; NOT gDW-normalized because no biomass/gDW reported, so cannot compute qS in mmol/gDW/h). No growth rate, doubling time, dilution rate, pH, or full medium recipe given in main text (ASW medium composition cited from refs 41,42). Back-flux MFA ratio f-/fnet = 1.5-2.5 permil of net AOB rate; electron-equivalent ratio butane-oxidation:sulfate-reduction = 1.04. Full ASW composition not in main text -> medium_composition_stated:false. Other three assigned papers had NO experimental prokaryote rates: PMC11525543 (sponge holobiont) reports only FBA-PREDICTED fluxes for uncultured symbionts N. spongiisocia (archaeon) and Z. abyssi (bacterium), no measured rates; PMC11536133 is an atmospheric-CH4 box-model/isotope study, no cultured prokaryote; PMC11532494 is a bioinformatic survey of bacterial classical deacylases (enzymology), no growth physiology.

records.notes: Measured specific growth rate μ=0.06 h−1 for wild-type strain S2 at 22°C, read from growth curves (Figure 1B). Doubling time calculated: td = ln2/μ = 0.693147/0.06 = 11.55 h. No substrate uptake or product secretion rates were measured in this paper (transcription-termination study). Medium is McF (composition cited from ref 28, not stored in-text) so medium_composition left empty. pH not stated. M. maripaludis is a hydrogenotrophic methanogen (H2/CO2 or formate) but substrate and methane rates were not quantified here. At 37°C S2 had a 'similar growth rate' to the mutant but no numeric μ was given (Supplementary Figure S1C), so no 37°C record was emitted.

records.cite: Lei Yue et al. The conserved ribonuclease aCPSF1 triggers genome-wide transcription termination of Archaea via a 3'-end cleavage mode. Nucleic Acids R
records.snip: ▽aCPSF1 exhibited markedly reduced growth at 22°C (μ = 0.035 h−1 versus 0.06 h−1 of S2, Figure 1B)
records.notes: Measured μ=0.035 h−1 for the Mmp-aCPSF1 expression-depleted strain at 22°C (Figure 1B). Doubling time calculated: td = ln2/μ = 0.693147/0.035 = 19.80 h. This is an engineered depletion strain (indigenous Mmp-aCPSF1 knocked out, expression driven by a repressible tetO system; ~3-fold lower aCPSF1 protein than 37°C culture), included because μ was directly measured; note reduced growth is a genetic-perturbation phenotype, not wild-type physiology. A ~2-fold longer bulk mRNA half-life (8.4 min vs 4.7 min for S2) was also reported but is not a growth-physiology rate. No uptake/secretion/MFA fluxes measured.

records.snip: CFUs that increased 3.6-fold between 4 and 48 h postinfection ... This corresponds to an estimated mycobacterial doubling time of 24 h, which matches our experimental data based on CFUs
records.notes: Only experimental prokaryote physiology in this paper is an intracellular growth rate. Reported: CFU increased 3.6-fold between 4 and 48 h p.i. (elapsed t=44 h). Calculated doubling time td = 44 h / log2(3.6) = 44 / 1.848 = 23.8 h approx 24 h (matches the paper's stated ~24 h estimate). mu = ln2/td = 0.6931/23.8 = 0.0291 /h. No defined medium (intracellular macrophage environment); medium_composition not stated. NO experimental uptake or secretion rates are reported: all substrate-uptake/utilization values (coutilization of up to 33 nutrients) are in silico predictions from flux balance analysis on a combined genome-scale macrophage+M. tuberculosis model constrained by dual RNA-seq, hence NOT experimental and deliberately omitted from uptake_rates/secretion_rates. Temperature 37 C assumed from standard THP-1 macrophage infection culture; pH not stated.

sec.method: Reported volumetric productivity = final titer 35.1 g/L over 41.7 h fed-batch; paper states 'productivity of 0.84 g L-1 h-1'. Cross-check: 35.1/41.7 = 0.842 g/L/h.

records.snip: After cultivation for 41.7 h, the pcnB i - acrD i - fadR + strain achieved a remarkable FFAs production of 35.1 g L-1, with a productivity of 0.84 g L-1 h-1 and a yield of 0.20 g g-1 glycerol. ... Fermentation temperature was set at 30 C an
records.notes: Metabolic-engineering (CRISPRi) paper, NOT a 13C-MFA study; no MFA fluxes. Only measured extracellular physiology rate is the FFA volumetric productivity. Titer=35.1 g/L FFA at 41.7 h in a 5 L bioreactor (1.8 L working vol), fed-batch, 30 C, pH 7 (controlled with 6 N NH4OH). Volumetric FFA productivity reported = 0.84 g/L/h; arithmetic check 35.1 g/L / 41.7 h = 0.842 g/L/h (matches). Product/substrate yield reported = 0.20 g FFA per g glycerol (glycerol is the carbon source; batch 15 g/L glycerol + fed 500 g/L glycerol feed). NO growth rate (mu), doubling time, biomass (gDW) time course, or specific substrate uptake rate (qS in mmol/gDW/h) is reported, so uptake_rate per gDW is NOT derivable and left is_experimental:false/null. Precursor titers reported only as endpoint amounts (mg/L, no rate): CF baseline 747 mg/L; pcnB_956 2916 mg/L; pcnB^i 2992 mg/L; pcnB^i-acrD^i-fadR^+ shake-flask 5105 mg/L (all in modified M9, glass-tube/shake-flask, 30 C, 250 rpm, 40 h post-IPTG, glycerol 30 g/L or glucose 30 g/L for Fig.4) - not emitted as separate records because no rate is derivable beyond a crude titer/time and no biomass data. Comparative titers cited from other organisms (Y. lipolytica 9.67 g/L, P. pastoris 23.4 g/L, S. cerevisiae 33.4 g/L, O. polymorpha 38.2 g/L, R. opacus 50.2 g/L) are literature citations, not measured here.

records.snip: DIC -> POC 2097.6 (day) / 66.4 (night) pmol colony-1 h-1; N2 -> PON 212.3 (day) / 0.9 (night); DIC -> Single-cell C fixation 0.20 ± 0.03 d-1 (day); N2 -> NH4+ 19.4 ± 14.0 (7.3-41.2); Oxygen production 2868 ± 2333 (light); Oxygen respiration
records.notes: Field-sampled Trichodesmium (marine cyanobacterium, prokaryote) colony incubations; NOT a GEM/13C-MFA cultivation study, and rates are per-COLONY (pmol colony-1 h-1), NOT biomass-normalised to gDW, so uptake/secretion are not directly GEM-constraint units. GROWTH RATE: single-cell C-fixation-based specific growth rate reported as 0.20 ± 0.03 d-1 (daytime) and 0.0012 ± 0.0006 d-1 (night) (Table 3, footnote: derived from Table 3 of Eichner et al. 2019 [ref 23], corrected for controls). Converted to per-h: 0.20 d-1 / 24 = 0.00833 h-1. Doubling time td = ln2/mu = 0.6931/0.20 d-1 = 3.466 d = 3.466 x 24 = 83.2 h. N2-fixation-based single-cell growth rate 0.03 ± 0.02 d-1 (day). Daytime C growth rate stated to be ~6x N growth rate. All listed pmol colony-1 h-1 net rates are daytime (6AM-6PM) values with night values noted in parentheses; N/D = not detectable (nitrification, denitrification, N2O from most pathways). Nitrate/nitrite reduction net rates (also measured, potential rates): NO3- -> NO2- 5.7 (day)/4.8 (night); NO3- -> NH4+ 5.0/10.9; NO2- -> NH4+ 1.6/1.1; residual NO3- consumption 65.7/67.8 pmol colony-1 h-1 — these are 'potential/net' rates because DIN was enriched 55-94% by 15N additions (authors' caveat), so only N2/C fixation and ammonium release are treated as true in-situ rates. O2 fluxes originally 0.039 ± 0.032 nmol cm-2 s-1 (light) and -0.024 ± 0.019 (dark) per unit colony surface, normalised to whole colony as production 2868 / respiration 1751 pmol colony-1 h-1. Medium = natural filtered seawater (salinity 34.4, 27°C in situ); full chemical composition not stated. POC ~ (Table 2) and cells ~6000 per colony (Vm normalisation used 6000 cells). No CO2/formate/ethanol/organic-acid secretion (photoautotroph).

records.snip: we performed pure culture growth experiments with 10 mM glucose as the electron and carbon source. Gas chromatography demonstrated that H2 rapidly accumulated in the headspace during cultivation with glucose, resulting in H2 concentrations
records.notes: Qualitative physiology paper; NO gDW-normalized uptake/secretion rates, NO measured specific growth rate, and NO biomass (gDW/OD) reported for R. cohabitans, so qS and qP cannot be computed. Only headspace H2 accumulation (ppmv) was measured by GC. Medium: R. cohabitans HM grown in bicarbonate-buffered freshwater medium pH 7.0 + 0.5 mM Na2S + 10 mM glucose + 0.1% yeast extract, 30 C, stationary, anaerobic (Methods 'Bacterial cultures and cultivation'). Base freshwater medium salt composition is cited from reference [5] (Bergmann/Meckenstock) and is NOT reproduced verbatim in this article. A speculative doubling time of ~13 days (shortening to ~11 days with necromass recycling) is discussed in the Discussion, but that value is for Desulfobacterium N47 from a hypothetical exponential-growth argument (10% inoculum -> full density in 6 weeks), NOT a measured R. cohabitans rate, so it is not encoded as growth_rate/doubling_time here. Ethanol and acetate are genome-predicted endproducts, not quantified. No isotopic/13C-MFA performed.

records.notes: R. thiooxydans FW510-R12 monoculture, anaerobic batch on 20 mM acetate + 10 mM NaNO3, 30C, pH 7.2, N2:CO2 80:20 headspace. Measured (experimental) consumption of NO3- and acetate and accumulation of N2O were quantified by ion chromatography and GC over time; numeric substrate uptake / product secretion rates (mmol/gDW/h) were computed for the ModelSEED/COBRApy GEM but the values reside in the GitHub repo (github.com/baliga-lab/3H11_R12_SynCom) and are NOT stated in the article text, so rate=null. Growth measured by OD600; max specific growth rate plotted (Fig 1F/2A) but no per-hour number in text. R12 encodes narG/nirK/norB/nosZ but nosZ is non-functional (cannot reduce N2O) -> incomplete denitrifier, hence N2O accumulation. Yield expressed only as OD600-vs-mM slope (0.033 for NO3-, 0.032 for NO2-). Full medium recipe cited from ref [10].

records.notes: R12+3H11 two-member SynCom, anaerobic batch, 20 mM acetate + 10 mM NaNO3, 30C, pH 7.2. Complete denitrification (NO3- -> N2) achieved via exchange of NO2- and NO between members. Experimentally, SynCom showed higher max growth rate and faster NO3- reduction than either monoculture (measured by OD600 and ion chromatography). Model (GEM + Monod kinetic) uptake/production rates for acetate, NO3-, NO2- were derived from these time-courses but numeric values reside in the GitHub repo / figures, not the article text -> rate=null. N2O off-gassing occurred only at high NO3- (proportional to initial NO3-, above ~20 mM) and high 3H11 inoculum fraction, driven by NO2- toxicity (inflection ~7.5 mM NO2-). Steady-state community composition ~65% 3H11 / 35% R12 by unique-peptide proteomics.
records.method: reported (measured by OD600; SynCom maximum growth rate was significantly greater than either monoculture [Fig 1F, Welch t-test]; numeric per-hour value not in text)

records.notes: 13C-tracer/metabolomics paper; NO absolute rates (mmol/gDW/h), growth rate, doubling time, or yields reported; cell numbers standardized to 1x10^10 CCU. Fig 5 depletion/secretion values are GC-MS relative abundances vs t=0 (lactate >5-fold at 30 min is the only semi-quantitative secretion number); 13C values are % enrichment (PPP >50%). M. gallisepticum = glycolytic/fermentative homolactic (secretes lactate), opposite of M. bovis (consumes lactate). All rates experimental but directional/relative; no derived qS possible.

records.strain: NCM3722 background; nonmotile engineered derivative NH3 (delta-fliC + lac operator deletion); measured strain designated LO1 (mutual-repression bistable-switch circuit)
records.cite: Chu et al. Colony pattern multistability emerges from a bistable switch. Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences of the United States of Ameri
records.snip: over a 14-h cultivation period, corresponding to approximately 32 generations (doubling time ~ 26 min) ... maximal growth rates range from 0.65 h-1 to 1.60 h-1 (SI Appendix, Table S7)
records.notes: Synthetic-biology colony-patterning study; only growth-physiology parameter usable for GrowthDB is the batch specific growth rate. E. coli NCM3722-derived strain LO1 grown in MOPS glucose defined medium, aerobic batch, 37 C, pH 7.4. Reported maximal batch growth rate (lambda_s) spans 0.65-1.60 h-1 across different carbon/nitrogen substrates (per-substrate values in SI Table S7, not in XML). This record uses the fastest (glucose-type) condition: doubling time ~26 min reported for the Fig 4 single-cell tracking experiment. mu = ln2/td = 0.6931/(26/60 h) = 0.6931/0.4333 h = 1.600 h-1, matching the stated upper bound 1.60 h-1; doubling_time_h = 26/60 = 0.4333 h. No substrate uptake, product secretion, or 13C-MFA fluxes are reported (glucose consumption enters only qualitatively via lambda_s). Other lambda values (e.g. 0.65 h-1 lower bound) correspond to poorer substrates not individually specified in main text. Exact glucose concentration and full trace-metal (NH4)6Mo7O24 value truncated/not stated in extracted XML; medium base is the Neidhardt (1974) MOPS formulation cited in Methods.
records.method: reported (doubling time ~26 min stated in text); mu cross-checked by calculation

records.snip: After 13 h of culture, around 29% of glyceraldehyde-3-P and around 25% of dihydoxyacetone phosphate (the products of the conversion of tagatose-1,6-bisphosphate by gatZ aldolase) were labelled with 13C ... Approximately 23% of 3-phosphoglyc
records.notes: This is a gut-microbiome/host-genetics Nature paper, NOT a dedicated GEM/13C-MFA study, but its Methods section 'Determining the metabolic flux ratios from 13C experiments by GC-MS' provides genuine EXPERIMENTAL prokaryote flux-tracer data. Strain 4-8-110 (OTU476-like) cultured in 6 ml GAM medium + 1 g/L GalNAc (13C-[UL-13C6] tracer vs 12C control), anaerobic glovebox (10% H2/10% CO2/80% N2), 37C, 13 h, triplicate. GC-MS-measured 13C-enrichment of central-carbon metabolites: GAP ~29%, DHAP ~25%, 3PG ~23%, lactate ~17% (mapping the GalNAc->tagatose-6-P->tagatose-1,6-bisP->GAP/DHAP->lower glycolysis->lactate pathway via GatZ aldolase). These are FRACTIONAL LABELLING proportions, NOT net fluxes and NOT rates in mmol/gDW/h; recorded in mfa_fluxes with units '% of pool labelled'. NO substrate uptake rate, NO product secretion rate (only lactate 13C-enrichment), and NO growth rate in mmol/gDW/h or per h are reported. Growth timing: OTU476-like strains reach end of exponential phase at ~11 h and were cultured 13 h; E. coli reference reached end of exponential phase at ~4 h; OD600 measured by spectrophotometer but no OD-vs-time series or mu given, so no growth_rate_per_h derivable (no arithmetic possible). pH not stated (GAM ~pH 7). Medium is complex/undefined (GAM), so medium_composition_stated=false; the only defined addition is GalNAc at 1 g/L. No numbers invented.

records.snip: Eleven amino acids were confirmed to support effective phosphorus release, with the highest rates observed for aspartate (21.3 mg P/g MLSS/h) and glutamate (18.5 mg P/g MLSS/h), followed by asparagine (12.9 mg P/g MLSS/h) and glutamine (10.
records.notes: Enrichment co-culture (not a pure isolate); SCELSE-1 is the predominant PAO (~45%). Measured values are anaerobic phosphorus-release RATES per g MLSS (mixed liquor suspended solids), verbatim from Results, used to validate GEM predictions of amino-acid utilization. Eleven amino acids supported P release but only these four highest were quantified in main text. No growth rate, no per-gDW uptake, no medium composition in main text (SI). No arithmetic performed; all values quoted directly.

records.snip: we enriched PAOs using glucose as the carbon source for 190 days... the relative abundance of a MAG closely related to Ca. Accumulibacter proximus BATAC285 reaching 5.7%... Batch test with glucose as a carbon source showed highly linked P r
records.notes: Enrichment co-culture; Ca. Accumulibacter only 9.7% genus-level (BATAC285-related MAG 5.7%), so measured values reflect the mixed community not a pure isolate. Reported glucose uptake (20.36 mg TOC/L) and P release (8.0 mg/L) are AMOUNTS in a batch test, not per-time or per-biomass rates; cannot derive mmol/gDW/h without biomass and time (not given). Established to resolve that GEMs predicted glucose use but PTS transporter ptsG absent and no glucose use in short-term batch experiments; long-term (190 d) enrichment did show glucose-linked P release. Values quoted directly; no arithmetic performed.

records.snip: wild-type strain JQ135 started to degrade DPA after a 12 h lag phase and completely removed the added 0.6 mM DPA within 8 h ... cells were further cultivated in 100 ml sterilized MSM containing 0.6 mM DPA on a rotary shaker (30 C, 180 rpm)
records.notes: Substrate uptake computed from explicit paper numbers: 0.6 mM DPA consumed within 8 h => 0.6/8 = 0.075 mM/h. 12 h lag before onset (not counted in active rate). No biomass (gDW) so cannot convert to mmol/gDW/h; no growth rate or yield reported. T=30 C, 180 rpm shaking, MSM per ref 23 (full composition not stated in this paper). Organism Alcaligenes faecalis JQ135 (Betaproteobacteria). Regulation/genetics paper, not a GEM/MFA study.

records.med: Human decidua tissue (ex vivo infection model)
records.cite: Lambert, Clara et al. The double-edged role of FASII regulator FabT in Streptococcus pyogenes infection. Nature Communications (2024). DOI:10.1038/s41
records.snip: the WT strain grew with a doubling time of roughly 200 min whereas the mFabT strain did not grow
records.notes: Growth rate calculated from reported approximate doubling time: td ~200 min = 3.33 h; mu = ln2/td = 0.693/3.33 = 0.208 /h. Value is explicitly 'roughly 200 min', so approximate. Growth is on human decidua tissue (host infection model), not a defined prokaryote medium, so not directly usable as a GEM uptake/secretion constraint. Companion condition: the mFabT (FabT H105Y) mutant did NOT grow on decidua (no numeric rate). Metabolomic 'consumption' of hexoses and amino acids (Asn, Ile, Lys, Ser) by mFabT vs WT was reported only as relative/differential abundances in conditioned supernatants (Fig 5, Suppl Table 5), with no absolute quantitative uptake rates and no biomass normalization, so no uptake_rate records could be derived. In standard THY / THY-Tween lab medium WT and mFabT grow similarly but no numeric doubling time/mu is given.

records.snip: Average uptake rates of glutamate and gluconate ... resulted in 0.10 and 0.08 mmol/gCDW*h at 0°C and 0.47 and 0.62 mmol/gCDW*h at 15°C. ... at 0°C the results of this simulation (0.021h) were in line with the measured growth rates (0.027h).
records.notes: Cold-adapted marine bacterium, GEM (constraint/validation) paper. Two carbon sources co-consumed (glutamate + gluconate), both experimental via 1H-NMR of spent medium. Uptake rates computed by authors as qS = mu/Yxs between T1(141 h) and T3; biomass from OD600 with scaling factor 0.66 g/L/OD at 0°C. Reported measured mu at 0°C = 0.027 h^-1 (T1-T3, used for FBA comparison; FBA predicted 0.021 h^-1). Whole-growth-period average mu = 0.016 h^-1 (s.d. 6.4e-05). Doubling time computed td = ln2/0.027 = 25.7 h. Medium (GG/Schatz) composition cited from ref 47, not restated -> composition empty. No excreted metabolic products reported (substrates depleted, no overflow secretion measured).
records.method: reported (measured growth rate between T1 and T3 used to constrain/validate FBA; whole-period average mu was 0.016 h^-1)

records.cite: Volke DC et al. Cofactor Specificity of Glucose-6-Phosphate Dehydrogenase Isozymes in Pseudomonas putida Reveals a General Principle Underlying Glycol
records.snip: Because growth on ribose of wild-type P. putida was slow (μ ∼ 0.06 h−1), we confirmed the mutual essentiality of G6PDH-A and G6PDH-B by growing the ΔzwfAB strain on M9 minimal medium agar plates containing ribose for 180 h
records.notes: Experimental specific growth rate stated verbatim in text: μ ≈ 0.06 h−1 for WT KT2440 on ribose in M9 (approximate, prefixed '∼'). doubling_time = ln2/μ = 0.6931/0.06 = 11.55 h (calculated). Carbon: 'M9 minimal medium ... supplemented with 20 mM citrate, 20 mM glucose, 24 mM ribose, 20 mM fructose, 40 mM glycerol, or 20 mM gluconate ... 120 mM total carbon'. Base M9 salt composition not given in text (cited to reference 24). Growth rates 'determined by fitting the temporal changes in OD600 to the exponential growth model' (n=3). No experimental substrate-uptake or product-secretion rates and no own 13C-MFA reported in this paper (paper explicitly contrasts steady-state 13C-MFA with its own batch growth); enzyme kcat/Km values reported are protein-level, not organism physiology.

records.snip: Nearly all glucose was converted into D-lactate with a titer of 140.92 g/L and a yield of 99% by the recombinant strain ZMB6 (Fig. 4c, Supplementary Table 2).
records.notes: Experimental fermentation of engineered Z. mobilis ZMB6 on glucose in RM rich medium. Reported as titer (140.92 g/L) and product yield (99% = 0.99 g D-lactate / g glucose). No specific growth rate, doubling time, or per-gDW uptake/secretion rate given in main text (kinetic time-course in Supplementary Table 2, not in XML). RM base composition (10 g/L yeast extract, 2 g/L KH2PO4) taken from Methods (RMG5 definition minus its 50 g/L glucose); glucose was varied to high concentration to reach 140.92 g/L product. Rates are volumetric (g/L), not per-biomass.

derived: 5.03 g/L / 376.36 g/mol = 13.36 mmol/L; /28 h = 0.477 mmol/L/h

records.snip: Finally, in a 5-L bioreactor, the performance of the engineered Gt-09 strain produced riboflavin with a final titer of 5.03 g/L within 28 h ... which represents the highest riboflavin titer achieved in thermophilic P. thermoglucosidasius.
records.notes: Genetic-tool / metabolic-engineering paper, NOT a GEM/MFA physiology paper. Only PRODUCT TITERS are reported for engineered riboflavin strains; no substrate uptake rate, no O2 rate, and no numerical specific growth rate (mu) are given, and no per-biomass (gDW) normalisation is possible because biomass at harvest is not reported. Growth was only 'monitored by OD600' (EnSpire plate reader) with no mu value stated. Riboflavin MW = 376.36 g/mol used for molar conversion. Derived volumetric rates: 5.03 g/L / 28 h = 0.1796 g/L/h; 5.03/376.36 = 13.36 mmol/L, /28 h = 0.477 mmol/L/h (these are VOLUMETRIC, not specific mmol/gDW/h, and are marked is_experimental:false because they are computed, not measured). Shake-flask endpoint titers for earlier strains (no fermentation time given, so no rate derivable): Gt-06 = 421 mg/L, Gt-08 = 521 mg/L, Gt-09 = 719 mg/L (all in 250 mL shake flasks, 250 rpm, 60 C). Fermentation medium composition is only in Supplementary Method 1 (not in the retrieved full text), so medium_composition_stated:false. Bioreactor temperature/pH/aeration not stated in full text (shake-flask fermentations were 60 C). Record retained for completeness of an engineered-prokaryote secretion observation; low confidence and NOT suitable as a quantitative GEM exchange-flux constraint.

Growth quantified by Growthcurver (mu, lag, doubling time) but numeric mu/td are in source-data/figures, not article text, so growth_rate_per_h/doubling_time_h left null. No mmol/gDW/h substrate-uptake or product-secretion rates reported. 13C-methyl DMSP labelling of SAM (41.6%) and MTA (47.9%) are isotope abundances (M+1), not MFA fluxes. Cellular C1 requirement estimate (190.1 amol C1 and 23,800 amol ATP per cell duplication) is a model-based calculation adapted from E. coli (ref. 32) and the P. inhibens metabolic model (ref. 33), not a measured rate.

Glycolytic carbon on glucose is channeled primarily through the Entner-Doudoroff pathway (6PG -> pyruvate + GAP) toward the TCA cycle.

records.snip: Growth rates on 100 mM C glucose as the sole carbon source... our model must produce a net glycolytic flux at 66% of glucose uptake rate (14)... During growth on glucose, high glycolytic carbon flux was channeled primarily through the Entne
records.notes: Glucose (glycolytic substrate) condition for wild-type P. putida KT2440. This is a 13C-kinetic-isotope-tracing + quantitative-metabolomics + proteomics study of the GAPDH node, NOT a rate-quantification MFA paper. NO numeric measured uptake/secretion (mmol/gDW/h) or numeric mu is present in the extractable main text (grep: mmol=0, gDW=0, h^-1 absent). Growth rates were measured but appear only in figures/SI (Fig. 3C, SI Figs. S4/S6). Intracellular metabolites were quantified as umol gCDW^-1 (relative log2 fold-change GLC vs FER), not exchange fluxes. The only numeric flux (66% of J_G glycolytic) is CITED from ref 14 (Kukurugya 2019) as a model constraint, not measured here. Medium recipe, temperature and pH are in SI Appendix only, hence medium_composition_stated=false. No derived rate computed (no inputs available).
records.method: reported (measured; numeric value only in figures/SI, not in extractable text)

records.snip: Growth rates on 100 mM C ferulate as the sole carbon source. NG: no growth... a net gluconeogenic flux at 15% of the ferulate uptake flux (38) for the respective growth conditions... high carbon flux remained largely in the TCA cycle with m
records.notes: Ferulate (gluconeogenic substrate) condition for wild-type P. putida KT2440. Same caveats as the glucose record: NO numeric measured uptake/secretion or mu in extractable text; growth measured but in figures/SI only. Only numeric flux (15% of J_F gluconeogenic) is CITED from ref 38 as a model constraint, not measured here. Key biological finding: metabolic segregation - after 13C switching, glucose-derived carbons did not enter gluconeogenesis in ferulate-acclimated cells and ferulate-derived carbons did not enter lower glycolysis/TCA in glucose-acclimated cells, controlled by GapA (glycolytic, 22-fold up on glucose) vs GapB (gluconeogenic, unchanged). Medium/temp/pH in SI only -> medium_composition_stated=false. No derived rate computed.
records.method: reported (measured; numeric value only in figures/SI)

records.snip: Cultures of A. tumefaciens C58 were grown in a phosphate-buffered mineral salts media (M9, pH 7.2), with glucose or SQ (10 mM) as the sole carbon source. Cultures were incubated at 30 C (250 rpm)... SQ consumption is coincident with product
records.notes: Genuine experimental prokaryote growth condition: A. tumefaciens C58 grows robustly on 10 mM SQ as sole carbon source (M9, pH 7.2, 30 C, 250 rpm, batch, aerobic). NO numeric physiological rates are reported in the text: growth (OD600), SQ consumption ([SQ]) and sulfite production (Delta[sulfite]) are plotted vs time only in Fig. 1A, so no mu, doubling time, qS or qP could be computed without figure-digitized values (not permitted). SQ uptake is qualitative (coincident with sulfite release); sulfite is the excreted product (autooxidizes to sulfate); transient 13C-bicarbonate detected during exponential phase by 13C NMR on 13C-SQ. M9 salt composition not enumerated in this paper (only carbon source conc 10 mM stated), hence medium_composition_stated=false. A. tumefaciens also grew on sulfoquinovosyl glycerol (SQGro) and other sulfoquinovosides (Fig. S3) but no separate quantitative conditions given; no growth on PIPES, cysteic acid, or methanesulfonic acid. This is a biochemistry/structural paper (enzyme kinetics, crystallography) not a GEM/13C-MFA physiology study.

records.cite: Abdul R Sheik et al. In situ phenotypic heterogeneity among single cells of the filamentous bacterium Candidatus Microthrix parvicella. The ISME Journ
records.snip: In response to different substrates and temperature conditions tested in this study, the estimated Ca. M. parvicella growth rates ranged from 0.12 to 0.78 day-1, which are in agreement with those estimated using the total extended filament
records.notes: Prokaryote (filamentous actinobacterium) growth rates estimated at the single-cell level from newly assimilated 13C-oleic acid (Foster et al. 2011 approach), spanning the different substrate/temperature/oxygen conditions tested; reported as a range mu = 0.12-0.78 day-1. Converted to per-hour by /24: 0.12/24 = 0.005 h-1; 0.78/24 = 0.0325 h-1. Doubling times from td = ln2/mu: at 0.78 d-1, td = 0.693/0.78 = 0.888 d = 21.3 h; at 0.12 d-1, td = 0.693/0.12 = 5.78 d = 138.6 h. Authors note these agree with prior filament-length estimates (Tandoi et al. 1998; Rossetti et al. 2002). NO substrate uptake or product secretion rates in mmol/gDW/h are reported: single-cell assimilation is expressed only as 13C atomic percentage (relative enrichment) and as fraction of cells assimilating (13C-oleic acid: ~21-55% of cells; 13C-glycerol-3-phosphate: ~5-35% of cells; under alternating aerobic-anoxic: ~3-28% of cells), which are not convertible to metabolic-flux uptake rates. Bulk supernatant 13C substrate concentrations are in Supplementary Table S1 (not in main text). No MFA. Temperature-dependent assimilation showed two optima (activity at 4 C, decreasing to 20 C, rising 25-30 C, falling at 35 C); main incubations at 25 C. Medium is undefined activated-sludge matrix, not a defined medium.

records.snip: Flux values were normalized to a net CO2 uptake rate that was estimated from the growth rate and cell carbon content: 0.0042 h−1 × 45 mmol-C (g DW)−1 = 0.186 mmol-C (g DW)−1 h−1. ... The media was supplemented with 45 mM of both ammonium an
records.notes: 13C/2H INST-MFA study of chemolithoautotrophic anammox growth in a steady-state continuous membrane bioreactor. Growth rate mu = 0.0042 h-1 (stated by authors, consistent with reported SRT of 10.9 days: 1/(10.9 d x 24 h) = 0.00382 h-1; hydraulic retention 48 h gives hydraulic D = 1/48 = 0.0208 h-1, but biomass removal ~10%/day governs mu). doubling_time = ln2/mu = 0.6931/0.0042 = 165.0 h. Net CO2 uptake computed by authors as mu x cell C content = 0.0042 h-1 x 45 mmol-C gDW-1 = 0.186 mmol-C gDW-1 h-1 (is_experimental=false: derived, not directly measured; serves as MFA anchor = 100 in Fig. 4 normalized map). Individual intracellular net flux values are given only in Fig. 4 (image) normalized to CO2=100 and in Supplementary Datasets 4-5, not in machine-readable body text; captured here are the CO2 anchor, the 23% RuMP contribution to F6P (SSR 839.9 vs 988.7, 95% CI), and qualitative directions of oxidative TCA/oxidative PPP/WLP. Formate is directly ASSIMILATED (not fully oxidized) via WLP; acetate is NOT used by K. stuttgartiensis in situ (side-population activity). Anammox catabolism: NH4+ + NO2- -> N2; no per-biomass catabolic uptake/secretion rates tabulated in the main text (activity verified via 14N15N from 15N-nitrite). Trace-elements composition cited from ref 43, not enumerated here.

records.notes: Cultured deep-branching anaerobic thermophilic bacterium with a manually curated genome-scale model (GEM-i3DAC). Optimal growth: 70 C, pH 7.0, 2.5% NaCl, 20 MPa. Specific growth rate (mu, h-1) and growth yields WERE measured (Methods: OD600 via HACH DR5000, mu by linear regression of exponential phase; growth in triplicate) but NO numeric mu, uptake, or secretion rate values appear in the article text - they are only in figures (Fig 2c sulfide vs OD600; Fig 2d yields) and Source Data. Elemental S0 (1% w/v) reduced to H2S is the key measured secretion (qualitative here). Carbon: requires >=11 essential amino acids; glucose and pyruvate serve as additional C sources only with 20aa present. No rate arithmetic possible - no explicit numbers in text. Full TRM recipe referenced to external methods (ref 37/80), not fully listed here.

records.notes: CAVEAT: these are HOLOBIONT-level rates (per g sponge wet weight), driven by the uncultured gammaproteobacterial symbiont 'Ca. Taurinisymbion ianthellae', NOT rates of an isolated prokaryote culture and NOT normalized to microbial gDW - so they are not directly usable as GEM exchange constraints in mmol/gDW/h without biomass conversion. Included per exhaustive-capture mandate. Measured uptake (assimilation) rates: 1.38 nmol C/min/g and 0.74 nmol N/min/g sponge wet wt from 13C/15N-taurine EA-IRMS. Sulfate export reported as endpoint concentration (590 uM) not a rate; ~full stoichiometric recovery of 100 uM added taurine sulfonate as sulfate. Ammonium export inferred from 15N. No growth rate, doubling time, or pH reported; temperature 25 C, dark, stirred oxic seawater incubation. Symbiont also predicted to import DMSP (demethylation + cleavage) from metaproteogenomics - no rate.

records.snip: The highest titers of Hb and Mb were obtained at 15 h of fed-batch fermentation. The titers of S-Hb, C-Hb, B-Mb, and P-Mb reached 0.81, 0.82, 1.11, and 1.01 g L-1, equivalent to the productivities of 54.00, 54.67, 73.33, and 67.33 mg L-1 h-
records.notes: Metabolic-engineering / recombinant-protein production paper; the only quantitative experimental rates are VOLUMETRIC product productivities of recombinant hemoproteins in a 5 L fed-batch (BSM medium, gluconate-induced), NOT metabolic exchange fluxes and NOT biomass-normalized (no gDW). No specific growth rate (mu), no substrate uptake rate, no O2 uptake, no Yxs, and no MFA are reported (full methods and BSM base composition are in an unavailable SI Appendix). Arithmetic check of paper's productivities from titer/time: 0.81 g/L=810 mg/L / 15 h = 54.0 mg/L/h; 0.82 g/L=820/15=54.67; 1.11 g/L~1100/15=73.33; 1.01 g/L=1010/15=67.33 (all match reported values). Feed additives stated: sodium gluconate 40 g/L (inducer), ammonium sulfate 24 g/L, yeast extract 48 g/L. Temperature/pH/aeration not given in main text. A separate 5 L fed-batch of strain Heme-K2 gave a heme titer of 282.17 mg/L on day 6 (no per-hour productivity stated). Confidence low for GEM-constraint use because rates are volumetric protein productivities, not gDW-normalized metabolite exchange fluxes.

records.notes: Genome-scale reconstruction (iEL2243_2) paper for E. lenta. ONLY quantitative MEASURED growth-physiology value in the article body is the experimental specific growth rate: median 0.32 h-1 in EDM1 (growthcurver logistic fit, r reported as harmonic mean across replicates). Doubling time calculated: td=ln2/mu=0.6931/0.32=2.17 h. FBA-predicted max mu=0.96 h-1 (PREDICTED, not experimental; also 0.54 h-1 in agmatine-based EDM1, predicted). Model uptake constraints were DERIVED from defined-media concentrations via Cobra concToCellRate using approximate cell dry weight 3.3e-13 g and a fixed 1 mM/gDW/h cap for untargeted-metabolomics compounds -> these are constraints, NOT measured fluxes. Uptake/secretion of arginine, acetate, ornithine, citrulline are reported qualitatively (metabolomics shifts / FVA direction), so rate=null with is_experimental=true only for direction. Acetate depletion to LOQ observed experimentally in 1 mM (not 10 mM) EDM1 for 3 strains (DSM 2243, AB8n2, Valencia). A separate RNA-Seq energy-source experiment spiked reduced-carbon EDM1 with L-arginine to 86 mM, sodium acetate to 14.5 mM, or agmatine sulfate to 30 mM (final). Comparative modeling of 24 strains uses PREDICTED (FBA) growth, not measured. Conditions: anaerobic (2-5% H2, 20% CO2, bal. N2), 37 C, batch.

records.notes: Prokaryote = marine ammonia-oxidizing archaeon N. maritimus SCM1. Rates are CELL-SPECIFIC (per cell per day), NOT per gDW and NOT tied to a reported growth rate, so cannot be converted to mmol/gDW/h without biomass-per-cell data (not given). Measured Vmax: ammonia oxidation = nitrite production = 7.14 +/- 0.25 fmol N cell-1 d-1 (this single flux serves as both NH4+ uptake and NO2- secretion, 1:1 N stoichiometry); N2O production Vmax = 0.97 +/- 0.02 amol N cell-1 d-1. Apparent half-saturation Km(app): NO2- production 220 +/- 50 nmol/L NH4+; ammonia 132 nmol/L; O2 uptake 133 nmol/L (last two cited as previously determined for SCM1). N2O:NO2- Vmax ratio = 0.97 amol / 7.14 fmol = 0.97e-18 / 7.14e-15 = 1.36e-4 (derived arithmetic). Conditions: SCM medium, pH 7.8, 30 C, dark, batch, mid-late exponential phase. This is a kinetics/isotope paper, not a GEM/biomass-yield study; no growth rate, yield, or per-gDW flux reported.
